final_summary:
On August 6, 2015, a massive mine waste spill occurred at the Gold King Mine in San Juan County, releasing approximately 1 million gallons of acidic water containing sediment and metals into Cement Creek, a tributary of the Animas River, around 10:30 a.m. on Wednesday in Denver. The contaminated water flowed downstream, turning the Animas River orange-brown and prompting authorities to take swift action to protect public health and safety. 

According to the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA), the spill was caused by a team working on behalf of the agency, which was attempting to secure and consolidate a safe entry point into the mine to pump and treat the contaminated water. However, the operation unexpectedly triggered a large release of mine waste water into Cement Creek. The EPA stated that the project aimed to reduce metal pollution flowing out of the mine into Cement Creek.

As the toxic plume made its way through the Animas River, the city of Durango took measures to conserve water and prevent contamination. The city stopped pumping raw water from the Animas River, and residents were advised to conserve water until further notice. Local businesses, such as Fort Lewis College, were also affected, with the city ceasing to supply them with raw water for non-potable uses.

La Plata County Sheriff Sean Smith closed the Animas River to public use, including tubing, rafting, and kayaking, due to the uncertainty surrounding the toxins present in the water. The closure applied to the entire stretch of the Animas River in La Plata County and was set to remain in place until the river was deemed safe.

Meanwhile, Colorado Parks and Wildlife placed cages containing fish in the Animas River to monitor the potential impact on aquatic life. Peter Butler, co-coordinator of the Animas River Stakeholders Group, noted that the water contained high concentrations of iron, aluminum, cadmium, zinc, and copper, and possibly other metals like lead and arsenic. The pH level of the water dropped significantly, from 7.8 to 5.8, indicating a substantial increase in acidity.

As the plume continued downstream, it reached the New Mexico border, prompting officials in the state to take precautions. Farmington city officials shut down water-supply intake pumps to avoid contamination, and residents were advised to stay out of the river until the discoloration had passed. San Juan County officials also asked agricultural water users to shut off their intakes.

In Silverton, located upstream of the spill site, the water appeared to be unaffected, and the town's water source remained uncontaminated. However, the incident has sparked concerns among locals, with some questioning the EPA's handling of the situation and the potential consequences for the environment and human health. The EPA has pledged to investigate the cause of the spill and to continue monitoring the situation to ensure public safety.

reference: – Colorado's Animas River isn't always bright orange, but that's how it looks today after the EPA accidentally spilled a million gallons of mine waste into a tributary. Officials in San Juan County say state officials and the EPA were actually trying to access contaminated water at Gold King Mine in southwest Colorado on Wednesday when they "unexpectedly triggered a large release of mine waste water into the upper portions of Cement Creek," report Time and the Durango Herald. The acidic water—described by one witness as "the filthiest yellow mustard water you've ever seen"—contains sediment and metals that could irritate the skin, reports the AP. There's no risk to drinking water, but residents of communities along the river have been urged to cut back on water usage until the plume moves on. Yesterday the plume reached Durango, Colo., which had stopped pumping water out of the river on Wednesday. "It's really, really ugly," says a La Plata County official. "Any kind of recreational activity on the river needs to be suspended." The waste is now making its way to New Mexico, as the Animas River meets up with the San Juan River in that state before the San Juan joins the Colorado River in Utah. Officials have placed cages of fish in the river to identify any ill effects and are testing the water to identify the specific metals released. An expert guesses the water contains high concentrations of iron, aluminum, cadmium, zinc, and copper, based on previous contamination. It may also contain mercury, lead, and arsenic, he says, "but there's no evidence of that at this point." (One Texas town recently experienced a creepy water problem.)

final_summary:
On Saturday, a chaotic scene unfolded in the Silver Lake neighborhood of Los Angeles when 28-year-old Gene Atkins embarked on a violent crime spree, leaving a trail of destruction and tragedy in its wake. Atkins first shot his 76-year-old grandmother, Mary Elizabeth Madison, and a 17-year-old girlfriend at their shared residence in the 1600 block of East 32nd Street. He then kidnapped his girlfriend and fled the scene, prompting a high-speed car chase through the streets of Los Angeles.

Atkins, driving a 2015 Toyota Camry, used a LoJack system to evade capture, but ultimately crashed the vehicle into a light pole outside a Trader Joe's supermarket in the 2700 block of Hyperion Avenue in Silver Lake. Witnesses reported that Atkins exited the car and began firing at Los Angeles police officers, who were in hot pursuit. In response, the officers returned fire, striking Atkins in the arm and hitting 27-year-old Trader Joe's employee Melyda Corado, who was attempting to flee the scene.

Corado, a valued and beloved member of the Trader Joe's crew, was struck by a police bullet, not Atkins', as she was caught in the crossfire. She sustained a fatal injury, with the bullet entering her body after initially hitting her arm. Corado tragically succumbed to her wounds and collapsed behind the manager's desk inside the store. The Trader Joe's store remained closed on Monday, while a memorial of signs and flowers in memory of Corado continued to grow outside the building.

Los Angeles Police Department Chief Michel Moore confirmed that the officers involved in the incident were acting in self-defense and responded to the immediate threat posed by Atkins. However, the devastating outcome has left the community reeling, and the LAPD is grappling with the consequences of their actions. Moore expressed his condolences to Corado's family and acknowledged the pain and trauma inflicted upon all parties involved.

Atkins, who was subsequently arrested and charged with numerous crimes, including murder, attempted murder, and kidnapping, made his initial court appearance on Tuesday. His bail was set at $18.775 million, and his next court date is scheduled for August 14. The Trader Joe's company released a statement expressing their sorrow and shock over Corado's passing, stating that she was a valued and beloved member of their team. A GoFundMe page was established to support Corado's funeral expenses, raising nearly $25,000 as of midday Monday.

In the aftermath of the tragedy, the community came together to pay tribute to Corado, with a growing memorial outside the Trader Joe's store. The incident serves as a poignant reminder of the complexities and risks inherent in law enforcement work, as well as the importance of supporting those affected by such traumatic events.

reference: – Los Angeles Police Department Chief Michel Moore had the toughest of jobs Tuesday: to announce that the Trader Joe's employee killed during a shootout in Los Angeles on Saturday lost her life to a bullet fired by police, not the suspect. "I am truly sorry," Moore said about 27-year-old Melyda Corado's death. "As a father, as an individual, it is unimaginable, the pain of the Corado family, and everyone that knew her. And we share that pain today." He said the officers involved will forever wrestle with the choices they made, though he believes "my officers' actions were taken to defend themselves and in direct response to the deadly threat" and therefore were appropriate. An investigation into the officers' actions is ongoing. ABC News reports he also detailed a timeline of the crime, which was allegedly sparked by a feud between a man and his grandmother over his girlfriend staying at the grandmother's home. Moore said Gene Atkins, 28, shot his grandmother several times and ultimately led police on a chase that ended when his vehicle crashed into a pole outside the grocery store. Moore said Atkins fired at cops as he ran toward the entrance; Corado had come to the same entrance upon hearing the crash and was struck in her left arm in the process. The bullet entered her body. "Miss Corado ran back inside the store and collapsed behind the manager’s desk," Moore said, per the Los Angeles Times. CBS Los Angeles reports that, among other charges, Atkins faces one count of murder in connection with Corado's death.

final_summary:
In the United States of America, the President holds significant authority, serving a four-year term and being elected alongside a Vice President. According to Article II of the U.S. Constitution, each state appoints electors, equal in number to their combined Senators and Representatives, to cast votes for President and Vice President. These electors must meet in their respective states, voting by ballot for two candidates, with at least one not from the same state as themselves. The person receiving the most votes becomes President if they achieve a majority of the total number of electors, unless there's a tie, in which case the House of Representatives chooses the President. 

However, a recent development involving the Trump administration has raised concerns about the distribution of funds to various organizations. The U.S. Trump administration has allegedly frozen $125 million in funding for the United Nations Relief and Works Agency (UNRWA), which aids Palestinian refugees. This move comes amidst a review of U.S. aid to the Palestinian Authority, following the suspension of contacts between the Palestinian Authority and the Trump administration due to the recognition of Jerusalem as Israel's capital. 

This action has sparked concern among Western diplomats, who warn that freezing or cutting this funding would severely impact UNRWA's operations and potentially exacerbate the humanitarian crisis in Gaza. Meanwhile, Israeli officials, including Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, have expressed mixed views on the matter, with some preferring a gradual reduction in funding rather than a drastic cut. 

In contrast, a similar scenario unfolded in 1974, when President Richard M. Nixon faced impeachment proceedings in the U.S. Congress. The House Judiciary Committee drafted articles of impeachment against him, citing his role in covering up the Watergate scandal and obstructing justice. Specifically, they accused him of preventing the administration of justice, delaying and impeding investigations, and concealing evidence related to the break-in at the Democratic National Committee headquarters in Washington, D.C. 

Fast-forward to the present, and a similar debate surrounding presidential powers has emerged. One of Trump's lawyers, John Dowd, argued that the President cannot be found guilty of obstructing justice due to his position as the nation's top law enforcement officer, citing Article II of the U.S. Constitution. However, this claim has been met with skepticism, particularly given Trump's recent tweet suggesting that he knew former National Security Adviser Michael Flynn lied to the FBI. 

Dowd attributed the tweet to himself, stating it didn't imply obstruction, but rather highlighted the President's right to express his opinions on ongoing cases. This stance echoes Nixon's own defense during the Watergate scandal, where he claimed that "when the President does it, that means it is not illegal." The implications of these assertions raise questions about the limits of presidential power and the extent to which the President can influence investigations and the justice system, particularly in relation to the Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI).

reference: – The Trump attorney who says he penned the "sloppy" tweet that some say indicates a possible obstruction of justice isn't done making headlines. John Dowd spoke to Axios' Mike Allen, scoffing at criticism of the tweet regarding Mike Flynn, which suggested Trump knew the former national security adviser had lied to the FBI before Trump canned James Comey. "The tweet did not admit obstruction," Dowd says. "That is an ignorant and arrogant assertion." Plus, the Constitution has Trump's back, per Dowd. "[The] president cannot obstruct justice because he is the chief law enforcement officer under [the Constitution's Article II] and has every right to express his view of any case," he claims. Newsweek notes it's not clear which section of Article II Dowd refers to, though it speculates on one part involving the president seeking the opinion "of the principal Officer in each of the executive Departments, upon any Subject relating to the Duties of their respective Offices." Allen notes that with this pronouncement, Trump's legal team is "setting the stage" to keep Trump insulated from obstruction of justice or collusion charges in the Russia probe—meaning, Allen speculates, that his lawyers may be nervous charges are coming down the pike. But one of Barack Obama's former counsels notes "it is certainly possible" for Trump to have obstructed justice, and Allen points out Richard Nixon's Articles of Impeachment were jump-started with the assertion that Nixon "has prevented, obstructed, and impeded the administration of justice." But whether Trump can or can't be held responsible for such a transgression may be moot, Allen adds. "The one thing everyone agrees on is that the House of Representatives, with its impeachment power, alone decides what is cause for removal from office," he writes. "For now, at least, the House is run by Republicans."

final_summary:
In the small college town of Charlottesville, Virginia, a desperate search for 18-year-old University of Virginia student Hannah Graham continues, nearly two weeks after her disappearance on September 13. Charlottesville Police Chief Timothy J. Longo appealed to property owners in the city and surrounding Albemarle County to keep a lookout for any signs of Graham, urging them to report unusual findings such as tire tracks, clothing, or other potential evidence that may aid in her location.

The investigation has centered around Jesse Leroy Matthew Jr., 32, who is charged with abducting Graham with the intent to sexually assault her. Matthew, described as a 6-foot-2, 270-pound man with dreadlocks, was taken into custody on Wednesday in Galveston, Texas, after a widespread manhunt. He was found camping on the beach in the remote Bolivar Peninsula, a 20-square-mile barrier island off the coast of Galveston, where he had set up a tent and was using a fake name.

Matthew's arrest followed a tip from a resident who recognized him from television broadcasts, and a subsequent search of his vehicle revealed no immediate signs of Graham. However, police have collected evidence from Matthew's car and apartment, which they claim supports the charge against him. According to Longo, prosecutors decided to press charges after reviewing evidence from experts at the crime lab in Richmond.

Graham was last seen on the city's Downtown Mall, walking with Matthew shortly after 1 a.m. on September 13. Her friends reported receiving a text message from her at 1:06 a.m., just minutes before Matthew arrived at the upscale Tempo Restaurant, where he ordered drinks and danced with friends. However, according to the restaurant's owner, Brice Cunningham, Graham did not enter the establishment with Matthew, but rather waited outside for him before leaving with him on foot.

Cunningham's account contradicts earlier statements made by police, who claimed to have a witness placing Graham inside the bar with Matthew. The discrepancy highlights the complexities of the ongoing investigation, which has yielded few concrete leads. Despite the challenges, Longo expressed optimism that Graham will be found safely, stating, "I have hope, and I think Hannah's mom and dad have hope."

As the search for Graham continues, authorities in Galveston are working to determine whether Matthew had any connections to the area. While officials at Liberty from 2000 to 2002 and Christopher Newport in 2003, where Matthew attended school, declined to comment on his departure from the institutions due to privacy concerns, it is known that he played football at both schools.

Meanwhile, residents of the Bolivar Peninsula, where Matthew was arrested, say that the area is a common spot for people seeking solitude and anonymity. Beach maintenance worker Cliff Reichel noted that the peninsula is often visited by individuals who prefer to keep a low profile, and that authorities typically only intervene if someone is engaging in illicit activity. The area's reputation as a haven for those seeking refuge is underscored by its history, including the discovery of mass graves linked to serial killer Dean Corll in the early 1970s.

As the investigation unfolds, authorities are working to piece together the events surrounding Graham's disappearance and Matthew's actions in the days leading up to his arrest. On Thursday, September 25, 2014, in Gilchrist, Texas, Matthew waived extradition in a Galveston courtroom. With a $100,000 reward being offered for information leading to Graham's safe return, the community remains hopeful that she will be found soon.

history.draft:
In the small college town of Charlottesville, Virginia, a desperate search for 18-year-old University of Virginia student Hannah Graham continues, nearly two weeks after her disappearance on September 13. Charlottesville Police Chief Timothy J. Longo appealed to property owners in the city and surrounding Albemarle County to keep a lookout for any signs of Graham, urging them to report unusual findings such as tire tracks, clothing, or other potential evidence that may aid in her location.

The investigation has centered around Jesse Leroy Matthew Jr., 32, who is charged with abducting Graham with the intent to sexually assault her. Matthew, described as a 6-foot-2, 270-pound man with dreadlocks, was taken into custody on Wednesday in Galveston, Texas, after a widespread manhunt. He was found camping on the beach in the remote Bolivar Peninsula, a 20-square-mile barrier island off the coast of Galveston, where he had set up a tent and was using a fake name.

Matthew's arrest followed a tip from a resident who recognized him from television broadcasts, and a subsequent search of his vehicle revealed no immediate signs of Graham. However, police have collected evidence from Matthew's car and apartment, which they claim supports the charge against him. According to Longo, prosecutors decided to press charges after reviewing evidence from experts at the crime lab in Richmond.

Graham was last seen on the city's Downtown Mall, walking with Matthew shortly after 1 a.m. on September 13. Her friends reported receiving a text message from her at 1:06 a.m., just minutes before Matthew arrived at the upscale Tempo Restaurant, where he ordered drinks and danced with friends. However, according to the restaurant's owner, Brice Cunningham, Graham did not enter the establishment with Matthew, but rather waited outside for him before leaving with him on foot.

Cunningham's account contradicts earlier statements made by police, who claimed to have a witness placing Graham inside the bar with Matthew. The discrepancy highlights the complexities of the ongoing investigation, which has yielded few concrete leads. Despite the challenges, Longo expressed optimism that Graham will be found safely, stating, "I have hope, and I think Hannah's mom and dad have hope."

As the search for Graham continues, authorities in Galveston are working to determine whether Matthew had any connections to the area. While officials at Liberty from 2000 to 2002 and Christopher Newport in 2003, where Matthew attended school, declined to comment on his departure from the institutions due to privacy concerns, it is known that he played football at both schools.

Meanwhile, residents of the Bolivar Peninsula, where Matthew was arrested, say that the area is a common spot for people seeking solitude and anonymity. Beach maintenance worker Cliff Reichel noted that the peninsula is often visited by individuals who prefer to keep a low profile, and that authorities typically only intervene if someone is engaging in illicit activity. The area's reputation as a haven for those seeking refuge is underscored by its history, including the discovery of mass graves linked to serial killer Dean Corll in the early 1970s.

As the investigation unfolds, authorities are working to piece together the events surrounding Graham's disappearance and Matthew's actions in the days leading up to his arrest. On Thursday, September 25, 2014, in Gilchrist, Texas, Matthew waived extradition in a Galveston courtroom. With a $100,000 reward being offered for information leading to Graham's safe return, the community remains hopeful that she will be found soon.

reference: – Jesse Matthew, charged with the abduction of missing UVa student Hannah Graham, was found in Galveston, Texas, Wednesday on a "beach out in the boonies," as one Texan put it. After a tip, police spotted Matthew camped out on a sliver of sand on the remote Bolivar Peninsula—the site of a mass grave used decades ago by serial killer Dean Corll and accessible by a single road, the AP reports. Matthew is one of many to hole up on the spot (among others: famed French pirate Jean Lafitte, rumor has it), where some stay for months. "We get lots of weird folks," says a local. "It seems like a good place to seek refuge. It's kind of remote." More on the case from the Washington Post and the Daily Progress: Matthew initially said he was "George Carr," but police discovered his true identity when they ran his car's plates. Authorities have searched the car, beach, and sand dunes. The case has brought to light another accusation against Matthew. An official has confirmed he was accused of raping a woman at Liberty University in 2002, but she didn't proceed with the case. He was a student there at the time. "Basically, the woman was saying she hadn't consented and Matthew was saying she had," says an attorney with the Lynchburg Commonwealth Attorney's Office. Searchers have scoured much of Charlottesville, Va., and are moving on to surrounding farms and woodland. They've asked area real estate agents to inspect any vacant buildings or properties they represent, and have told the public to be on the lookout for Graham's iPhone 5S with a pink case or unexplained tire tracks. Authorities are also looking to identify some of Matthew's favorite fishing spots. Matthew has declined to fight his extradition, and Virginia police have been sent to retrieve him. He should arrive back in Charlottesville in a few days.

final_summary:
Maia Weinstock, the deputy editor of MIT News, has been using her creativity to bring attention to the often-overlooked contributions of women in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM) fields through her miniature LEGO figurines. Inspired by her love of space and the U.S. National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA), Weinstock's latest project, "The Women of NASA," has been selected to become an official LEGO set, celebrating the achievements of five trailblazing women who paved the way for future generations.

Weinstock's passion for promoting women in STEM began when she started creating LEGO likenesses of prominent scientists and engineers, including MIT Institute Professor Emerita Mildred Dresselhaus, Vice President for Research Maria T. Zuber, and Department of Chemical Engineering head Paula Hammond, the David H. Koch Chair Professor in Engineering. Her efforts aim to boost the visibility of female scientists and inspire young girls to pursue careers in STEM fields.

The "Women of NASA" set features five remarkable women who made significant contributions to the U.S. space program: Margaret Hamilton, a computer scientist who developed software for the Apollo missions; Mae Jemison, the first African-American woman in space; Katherine Johnson, who calculated and verified trajectories for the Mercury and Apollo programs; Sally Ride, the first American woman in space; and astronomer Nancy Grace Roman, a pioneer in the planning of the Hubble Space Telescope. Weinstock carefully curated the selection to represent a diverse group of women from various backgrounds, ages, and areas of expertise within NASA.

Weinstock's inspiration for the project came from her own experiences and observations. She notes that there is a lack of representation of women in STEM fields, both in media and popular culture. By creating LEGO figures, she aims to provide a fun and accessible way to educate people about the achievements of these women and challenge stereotypes. As she puts it, "I hope that 'Women of NASA' will be one little extra brick in the wall of trying to improve how women are perceived and shown in books, toys, and family programming."

The set's success is a testament to Weinstock's dedication to promoting women in STEM. With over 10,000 votes, her proposal was officially reviewed by LEGO, and the result is a set that will soon be available to fans worldwide. Weinstock's work has already caught the attention of some of her subjects, including MIT's Sangeeta Bhatia, who exclaimed, "Being immortalized in LEGO is probably the coolest thing that has ever happened to me."
anchors: The National Aeronautics And Space Administration

reference: – Mathematician Katherine Johnson was one of the women of NASA depicted in the Oscar-nominated film Hidden Figures, and now she's about to get her own Lego figure. Per MIT News, Johnson and four other women involved with the space agency over the years—Margaret Hamilton (computer scientist), Mae Jemison (first African-American woman in space in 1992), Nancy Grace Roman (pioneering NASA female exec and astronomer), and Sally Ride (the first US woman in space in 1983)—are set to be part of Maia Weinstock's "Women of NASA" Lego prototype, which was chosen from the Lego ideas submission website to be made into an actual set. Concepts for potential sets can be posted on the site, and after the public votes (Weinstock, the deputy editor of MIT News, got 10,000 votes for her idea), Lego then reviews eligible projects. Weinstock says she not only wanted to give a nod to the history of women in STEM, but also to "set an example" for both girls and boys that it isn't "strange or unusual" for there to be females working as astronauts, computer programmers, or any other science or technology expert. Lego says more details on Weinstock's set, including how much it will cost and its release date, should be out later this year or in early 2018. (Cambridge is offering a Lego professorship.)

final_summary:
In the rural town of Parsons, Tennessee, a chilling incident has left authorities scrambling to connect the dots between two disturbing cases. Holly Bobo, a 20-year-old nursing student and cousin of country music singer Whitney Duncan, vanished from her home on April 13, sparking a massive search effort. Her brother, Clint Bobo, described the harrowing moment when a man clad in camouflage clothing took his sister away at approximately 7:30 am.

Meanwhile, in a nearby town, Centerville, a similar encounter unfolded. Heather Sullivan, a local resident, recounted to NBC News how a tall, skinny man approached her outside her home at around 5:30 am, grabbing her arm as she stepped out of her car. The sudden movement caused her to drop a lamp, which shattered on the ground. Her boyfriend, who rushed out upon hearing the sound, allegedly scared off the suspect.

The eerie similarities between these incidents have raised suspicions among investigators. Mark Gwyn, director of the Tennessee Bureau of Investigation, acknowledged that they are exploring all possible connections between the two cases. "We're looking at all possibilities," he stated in a press conference. As the investigation unfolds, a substantial reward of $80,000 has been offered, with $50,000 contributed by the state, to encourage tips and information that could lead to Holly's safe return.

Area residents have joined forces to scour the woods surrounding both homes, searching for any clues that might shed light on the disappearances. A blood sample discovered near the scene of Holly's vanishing has been sent for analysis, although the results remain undisclosed. Amidst the uncertainty, Holly's family remains hopeful that their loved one is still alive. In a heart-wrenching appeal, her mother, Karen Bobo, pleaded with the public, "Holly, I love you. Please help her come home to us."

Click for more, including a video report.

final_summary:
Katherine Heigl, once known as the "rom-com queen" for her starring roles in numerous romantic comedies, has returned to the entertainment industry after a brief hiatus. She cited burnout and a desire to challenge herself creatively as reasons for stepping away from Hollywood in 2009. During her time off, Heigl reflected on her past choices, stating that while she loved romantic comedies, she may have overdone it, leading to a sense of monotony and a lack of personal growth as an artist. This self-evaluation ultimately led to her decision to reassess her career goals and seek a healthier work-life balance.

In an exclusive interview with Marie Claire, Heigl discussed her return to the industry, citing a newfound appreciation for her career and a commitment to finding a better balance between her professional and personal life. Her latest project, the TV series "State of Affairs," allows her to limit her episode count to 15 per season, enabling her to relocate to Utah and pursue her passions outside of acting.

In contrast, Ryan Gosling and Rachel McAdams, stars of the iconic romantic drama "The Notebook," faced their own challenges during filming in 2007. According to director Nick Cassavetes, Gosling requested that McAdams be removed from the set and replaced with another actress due to their strained working relationship. However, the tension between the leads eventually dissipated after a heated argument, which allowed them to continue working together more smoothly.

Interestingly, despite their tumultuous on-set dynamic, Gosling and McAdams developed a real-life romantic relationship, although Gosling himself seemed perplexed by how it began. Their relationship lasted several years before ending in 2007, with Gosling recalling that they parted ways after a fierce argument, drawing a metaphorical line in the sand.

Gosling and McAdams' on-screen chemistry in "The Notebook" remains iconic, with the film's release coinciding with the 13th Annual Screen Actors Guild Awards in Los Angeles, where they attended together. The event was captured by photographer Gabriel Bouys, who snapped a memorable photo of the couple on the red carpet. In a 2007 interview with The Observer, Gosling opened up about his rocky relationship with McAdams, stating that they "inspired the worst in each other" during filming.

reference: – If you thought Katherine Heigl's career was suffering because she's difficult to work with, well, allow the actress to set the record straight: It was actually doing too many romantic comedies that was the problem. "I had an amazing time. I love romantic comedies," she explains in a new interview with Marie Claire UK. "I was so stoked to be doing them. But maybe I hit it a little too hard. I couldn’t say no. There’s nothing wrong with them, but maybe I overloaded my audience. I should have done a superhero movie or a psychological thriller." That's when things changed for her. "This thing that was my best friend for a long time suddenly turned on me," she says, referring to the biz. "And I didn’t expect it. I was taken by surprise and angry at it for betraying me." But that's not the only reason she took a five-year hiatus from the limelight, she explains: Thanks to the plethora of rom-coms, "I stopped challenging myself. It became a bit by rote and, as a creative person, that can wear you down. That was part of why I took that time off, to ask myself, 'What do I want? What am I looking for?' and shut down all the noise." She's back now, with a new TV show. (Speaking of movie romances, a shocking interview out yesterday revealed that Ryan Gosling wanted Rachel McAdams kicked off The Notebook set.)

final_summary:
Syria has been ravaged by nearly five years of conflict, resulting in catastrophic consequences for its people and economy. According to the Syrian Centre for Policy Research (SCPR), the war has claimed the lives of approximately 470,000 individuals, a staggering figure that dwarfs the United Nations' previously cited estimate of 250,000 fatalities. This devastating loss represents 11.5% of the country's population, with 1.9 million people wounded and life expectancy plummeting from 70 in 2010 to 55.4 in 2015. The economic toll is equally dire, with overall losses estimated at £175 billion.

The SCPR's report highlights the disproportionate impact of the conflict on various sectors, including healthcare, education, and employment. Consumer prices have risen by 53%, and poverty has increased by 85% in 2015 alone. The displacement of 45% of the population, with 6.36 million internally displaced and over 4 million fleeing abroad, has exacerbated the humanitarian crisis. The report emphasizes that the international community has been slow to respond to the scale of the disaster, only intensifying its focus on human rights and dignity once the crisis began affecting developed countries.

In contrast to the UN's decision to cease documenting fatalities in mid-2014 due to limited access and diminished confidence in data sources, the SCPR conducted rigorous research on the ground across Syria to arrive at its estimate. The organization's report, titled "Confronting Fragmentation," underscores the need for a comprehensive understanding of the crisis, rather than focusing solely on the immediate effects of violence. The study reveals that indirect deaths, largely attributed to inadequate healthcare, lack of essential services, and displacement, will likely continue to rise.

As the conflict rages on, the situation in Aleppo remains particularly dire, with warnings of the city being severed from the rest of the country. The Syrian opposition is urging swift action to alleviate the plight of tens of thousands of civilians, while the International Red Cross reports that 50,000 people have fled the area in recent days. On Wednesday, US Secretary of State John Kerry met with his Russian counterpart, Sergei Lavrov, in Munich, where they discussed potential solutions to the crisis. However, the US has expressed reservations about Russia's proposal for a ceasefire on March 1, citing concerns that opposition forces could suffer irreparable losses before a ceasefire takes hold. Instead, the US has called for an immediate cessation of hostilities accompanied by unfettered humanitarian access to besieged areas. The Obama administration's strategy has been criticized for its complexity and perceived lack of resolve, with some lawmakers expressing frustration over the pace of progress in defeating the Islamic State and removing President Bashar al-Assad from power.

history.draft:
Syria has been ravaged by nearly five years of conflict, resulting in catastrophic consequences for its people and economy. According to the Syrian Centre for Policy Research (SCPR), the war has claimed the lives of approximately 470,000 individuals, a staggering figure that dwarfs the United Nations' previously cited estimate of 250,000 fatalities. This devastating loss represents 11.5% of the country's population, with 1.9 million people wounded and life expectancy plummeting from 70 in 2010 to 55.4 in 2015. The economic toll is equally dire, with overall losses estimated at £175 billion.

The SCPR's report highlights the disproportionate impact of the conflict on various sectors, including healthcare, education, and employment. Consumer prices have risen by 53%, and poverty has increased by 85% in 2015 alone. The displacement of 45% of the population, with 6.36 million internally displaced and over 4 million fleeing abroad, has exacerbated the humanitarian crisis. The report emphasizes that the international community has been slow to respond to the scale of the disaster, only intensifying its focus on human rights and dignity once the crisis began affecting developed countries.

In contrast to the UN's decision to cease documenting fatalities in mid-2014 due to limited access and diminished confidence in data sources, the SCPR conducted rigorous research on the ground across Syria to arrive at its estimate. The organization's report, titled "Confronting Fragmentation," underscores the need for a comprehensive understanding of the crisis, rather than focusing solely on the immediate effects of violence. The study reveals that indirect deaths, largely attributed to inadequate healthcare, lack of essential services, and displacement, will likely continue to rise.

As the conflict rages on, the situation in Aleppo remains particularly dire, with warnings of the city being severed from the rest of the country. The Syrian opposition is urging swift action to alleviate the plight of tens of thousands of civilians, while the International Red Cross reports that 50,000 people have fled the area in recent days. On Wednesday, US Secretary of State John Kerry met with his Russian counterpart, Sergei Lavrov, in Munich, where they discussed potential solutions to the crisis. However, the US has expressed reservations about Russia's proposal for a ceasefire on March 1, citing concerns that opposition forces could suffer irreparable losses before a ceasefire takes hold. Instead, the US has called for an immediate cessation of hostilities accompanied by unfettered humanitarian access to besieged areas. The Obama administration's strategy has been criticized for its complexity and perceived lack of resolve, with some lawmakers expressing frustration over the pace of progress in defeating the Islamic State and removing President Bashar al-Assad from power.

reference: – Russia has proposed a March 1 ceasefire in Syria, US officials say, but Washington believes Moscow is giving itself and the Syrian government three weeks to try to crush moderate rebel groups. The United States has countered with demands for the fighting to stop immediately, officials say. The talk of new ceasefire plans comes as the US, Russia and more than a dozen other countries meet in Munich to try to halt five years of civil war in the Arab country. The Guardian reports that according to a new study from the Syrian Center for Policy Research, the war has killed or injured 470,000 people—11.5% of the population—and has also lowered life expectancy from 70 to 55 and almost "completely obliterated" the country's infrastructure. The most recent Russian-backed offensive, near Aleppo, prompted opposition groups to walk out of peace talks last month in Geneva, while forcing tens of thousands of civilians to flee toward the Turkish border. Sources tell the AP that the US can't accept Russia's offer of a March 1 ceasefire because moderate opposition forces could suffer irreversible losses in northern and southern Syria before it ever takes hold. Defense Secretary Ash Carter, meanwhile, says NATO military authorities have been ordered to draw up plans for how the alliance could help shut down illegal migration and people smuggling across the Aegean Sea. Three NATO allies—Turkey, Germany, and Greece—requested alliance participation in an international effort to help end Europe's gravest migration crisis since World War II.

final_summary:
In West Virginia's capital city of Charleston, a state with a long history of struggling to regulate its coal and chemical industries, a major chemical spill occurred on Thursday, contaminating the water supply for over 300,000 people. The spill, which originated from a 40,000-gallon tank at a Freedom Industries facility along the Elk River, released 4-methylcyclohexane methanol (MCHM) into the water supply, prompting a do-not-use ban on tap water. The chemical, used in coal processing, was detected in the water supply on Thursday morning, and by Saturday, 169 people had sought medical attention for symptoms such as nausea, with 10 being admitted to hospital.

The spill has highlighted the state's lax regulatory environment, particularly regarding chemical storage facilities. West Virginia law does not require regular inspections of chemical storage facilities, unlike some other states. The last inspection of the Freedom Industries facility was in 1991, and the company is exempt from regular inspections and permitting due to its status as a chemical storage facility rather than a production facility.

Governor Earl Ray Tomblin acknowledged the need for reform, stating that the state needs to "give some serious thought to making some serious reform and valuing our natural resources over industry interests." Tomblin also announced plans to work with the State Department of Environmental Protection to tighten regulations on chemical storage facilities during the upcoming legislative session.

As of Sunday, efforts to clean up the spill showed promising signs, with tests conducted at a water treatment plant showing little to no traces of contamination. However, officials cautioned that the ban on tap water would not be lifted until consistent results were obtained and the state health department gave the green light. West Virginia American Water launched an online map to track which communities could resume using their tap water, and the company pledged to notify customers via phone calls when the ban was lifted in their area.

The spill has had significant economic and social impacts, with businesses forced to close and residents relying on bottled water for basic necessities. Local residents expressed frustration with Freedom Industries' response to the spill, feeling that the company was not transparent enough about the situation. The incident has sparked renewed debate about the state's reliance on the coal and chemical industries, with critics arguing that the risks associated with these industries outweigh the benefits.

On Sunday, Governor Tomblin stated that the state was "at a point where we can see a light at the end of the tunnel," but emphasized that the ban on tap water would not be lifted until further notice. As of Monday, schools in six counties remained closed, and officials continued to caution residents against drinking, bathing, or cooking with the tainted tap water. The state Department of Environmental Protection has ordered Freedom Industries to develop a plan to clean up the spill and any soil contamination within 24 hours.

reference: – The chemical spill that has left more than 300,000 people in West Virginia without water appears to be the product of lax regulation in a state where coal and chemical firms have long operated with little oversight, the New York Times finds. A document released over the weekend reveals state authorities were aware that the company responsible was storing up to a million pounds of the coal-cleaning chemical Crude MCHM at a riverside facility near a water treatment plant, but it's not clear whether the treatment plant knew about the risk, reports the Wall Street Journal. "We can’t just point a single finger at this company," says the director of the West Virginia Rivers Coalition, noting that the site of the spill hadn't been inspected since 1991. "We need to look at our entire system and give some serious thought to making some serious reform and valuing our natural resources over industry interests." A tap water ban is still in place in the Charleston region, where many schools and businesses remain closed, but authorities say they can "see the light at the end of the tunnel" and they expect the ban to be lifted within days, starting in downtown Charleston and other priority zones, NBC reports.

final_summary:
On Thursday, January 28, 2016, a 25-year-old woman named Lauren Mann, originally from Colorado, was found dead in her apartment in Vienna, Austria. Mann, who had been working as an au pair for a local family, had not shown up for work, prompting her employer to alert the police. Upon entering the apartment, police and firefighters discovered Mann's half-naked body on a mattress in the bedroom. 

According to Vienna police spokesman Thomas Keiblinger, the initial autopsy did not reveal the cause of death, leading investigators to await the results of toxicological tests. However, subsequent autopsies later confirmed that Mann died from suffocation. 

Mann, a graduate of the University of Colorado-Boulder's College of Music, class of 2012, had been living in Vienna for several years, working as an au pair while pursuing further studies. Her family described her as "very reliable, trusted, caring, and socially active." 

ABC News reported that police initially treated the case as a possible homicide due to the possibility of third-party involvement, although no suspects or arrests had been made at the time. CBS News also stated that police were treating the case as a homicide, citing the inability to exclude third-party involvement. As the investigation continued, police spoke with Mann's friends and neighbors, including Peter Kratochwill, who expressed surprise at the incident given the quiet and safe nature of the neighborhood. 

American authorities, including the U.S. Embassy in Vienna, provided consular assistance during the investigation. The U.S. government identified Mann as the victim, and officials from the University of Colorado-Boulder also released a statement expressing sadness over her passing. In addition, Mann's hometown of Paonia, Colorado, remembered her fondly, with local resident Lisa Heinritz recalling Mann's childhood dance lessons and describing her as a "happy little girl" with many friends.

reference: – Officials have released a name in the case of the American nanny found dead in her Vienna, Austria, apartment on Tuesday night, but beyond that, much remains unclear. ABC News reports Lauren Mann, 25, died by suffocation, though it notes that Vienna police rep Thomas Keiblinger earlier referenced a preliminary autopsy that found no indications of strangulation. NBC News echoes the report of suffocation, but CBS News and the AP report the autopsy was inconclusive, and their reports don't mention suffocation. CBS cites officials who on Thursday confirmed Mann was not strangled, shot, or stabbed. The case is being treated as a homicide, and an official tells NBC News "third party involvement cannot be excluded"; police say there are no suspects at this time. Keiblinger says it'll take a week or two for the toxicology report to be completed, which has CBS saying her death "could be [a] mystery for weeks." ABC reports Mann failed to pick up one of her charges from school on Monday, but her half-dressed body wasn't found until 6pm the following day. The Colorado native was a graduate of the University of Colorado-Boulder's College of Music.

final_summary:
In the lead-up to the end of 2011, the United States military is preparing to withdraw from Iraq, marking a significant shift in the country's security landscape. As part of this transition, the Obama administration plans to assume responsibility for training the Iraqi police, a task that will primarily be carried out by contractors. In October 2011, the U.S. State Department will take charge of this endeavor, utilizing a team of approximately 2,400 personnel stationed at the Baghdad embassy and other diplomatic sites, located in two new $100 million outposts.

To safeguard civilians in a country plagued by Al Qaeda-linked insurgents and Iranian-backed militias, the U.S. State Department intends to nearly double its private security guard contingent, increasing the number to as many as 7,000. These security contractors will be responsible for operating radars to detect enemy rocket attacks, searching for roadside bombs, flying reconnaissance drones, and staffing quick reaction forces to assist civilians in distress. They will also defend five fortified compounds across the country.

This unprecedented civilian-led operation is set to unfold in an environment fraught with danger, prompting concerns among seasoned diplomats and military experts. James Dobbins, a former ambassador, noted that the U.S. State Department has never before operated independently in such a perilous setting on a large scale. Despite these challenges, White House officials express confidence in the ability of the civilian team to maintain stability in Iraq.

However, some veterans of the Iraq conflict argue that a smaller military presence may be necessary to address ongoing security concerns. Ryan C. Crocker, who served as ambassador in Iraq from 2007 to 2009, emphasized the importance of strategic patience and flexibility in addressing the complex needs of the Iraqi partner. Military experts and Iraqi officials suggest that American troops may be required to perform tasks such as training Iraqi forces to operate and support newly acquired military equipment, protecting Iraq's airspace, and assisting in counterterrorism operations.

As the U.S. military reduces its troop levels to 50,000 by the end of August, the U.S. State Department is poised to expand its role in Iraq. The U.S. Embassy in Baghdad will become a hub for diplomatic efforts, with a focus on promoting economic growth and regional stability. The Obama administration views this transition as a crucial step towards a lasting peace in Iraq, despite acknowledging the risks associated with the withdrawal.

In Afghanistan, the U.S. is asking to remove a bulk of its 45,000 private contractors from the country. The U.S. military will continue to provide support to the Iraqi government through the end of next year, with a focus on training and capacity-building initiatives. As the United States transitions to a civilian-led approach in Iraq, the country remains a critical component of regional security, with the U.S. committed to preserving its investment in the country's stability and prosperity.

final_summary:
In a dramatic turn of events, Alan Miller McCarty, a 36-year-old resident of Milton in Santa Rosa County, faced a tumultuous trial in Daytona Beach, Florida, where he was ultimately found guilty on multiple charges related to his threats against a judge and others. On Thursday, McCarty's behavior became increasingly erratic, prompting the presiding judge, Matt Foxman, to relocate him to a separate room with one-way glass due to his persistent interruptions, insults, and crude language directed towards the judge, witnesses, and the entire judicial system. Despite being separated from the main courtroom, McCarty continued to scream and hurl expletives, including a particularly disturbing comment inviting Judge Foxman to engage in a lewd act.

The charges against McCarty stemmed from his phone calls to dispatchers and Circuit Judge Stasia Warren, whom he blamed for ruling against him in an earlier custody case. During these calls, McCarty made explicit threats to kill Judge Warren, stating that he would "execute" her in the street. These threats left Judge Warren visibly shaken, and she testified that she feared for her safety. 

Assistant Public Defender Ryan Belanger attempted to downplay McCarty's actions, suggesting that his client was simply a "very squeaky wheel" trying to get attention due to feeling overwhelmed by the loss of his children, who had been living in Texas since 2007. However, prosecutors countered that McCarty's words carried significant weight and posed a genuine threat to the integrity of the judicial system.

Following a relatively brief deliberation period, the jury delivered a guilty verdict on two counts of threats or extortion and two counts of corruption by threat against a public official. This decision came with a potential sentence of up to 40 years in prison, with a sentencing hearing scheduled for October 12th of this year. In addition to these charges, McCarty also faces further accusations of threatening employees at Stewart-Marchman-Act Behavioral Healthcare.

As McCarty awaits sentencing, he remains incarcerated at the Volusia County Branch Jail, where he has been placed on suicide watch. His behavior during the trial raised concerns among court officials, who maintained a high level of professionalism despite his outbursts. Following the conviction, State Attorney R.J. Larizza released a statement condemning McCarty's actions as "chilling and offensive," emphasizing the gravity of threatening judges and undermining the foundations of the justice system.

Ultimately, McCarty received a 20-year prison sentence, which will provide ample opportunity for reflection on the severity of his actions.

The charges against McCarty stemmed from his phone calls to dispatchers and Circuit Judge Stasia Warren, whom he blamed for ruling against him in an earlier custody case. During these calls, McCarty made explicit threats to kill Judge Warren, stating that he would "execute" her in the street. These threats left Judge Warren visibly shaken, and she testified that she feared for her safety. 

Assistant Public Defender Ryan Belanger attempted to downplay McCarty's actions, suggesting that his client was simply a "very squeaky wheel" trying to get attention due to feeling overwhelmed by the loss of his children, who had been living in Texas since 2007. However, prosecutors countered that McCarty's words carried significant weight and posed a genuine threat to the integrity of the judicial system.

Following a relatively brief deliberation period, the jury delivered a guilty verdict on two counts of threats or extortion and two counts of corruption by threat against a public official. This decision came with a potential sentence of up to 40 years in prison, with a sentencing hearing scheduled for October 12th of this year. In addition to these charges, McCarty also faces further accusations of threatening employees at Stewart-Marchman-Act Behavioral Healthcare.

As McCarty awaits sentencing, he remains incarcerated at the Volusia County Branch Jail, where he has been placed on suicide watch. His behavior during the trial raised concerns among court officials, who maintained a high level of professionalism despite his outbursts. Following the conviction, State Attorney R.J. Larizza released a statement condemning McCarty's actions as "chilling and offensive," emphasizing the gravity of threatening judges and undermining the foundations of the justice system.

Ultimately, McCarty received a 20-year prison sentence, which will provide ample opportunity for reflection on the severity of his actions.

reference: – Florida's Alan McCarty has made clear he does not like judges, and he will spend the next 20 years in prison as a result. The 36-year-old received the 20-year sentence this week—along with an extra 10 days for contempt of court, a penalty more serious than it sounds—for calling 911 last year and promising to kill a judge he mistakenly believed had ruled against him in a custody case, reports the Daytona Beach News Journal. During Wednesday's sentencing in front of a different judge, Matt Foxman, McCarty had to be removed from the courtroom as Foxman listed his offenses. “What a bunch of (expletive) lies you stupid piece of (expletive),” McCarty yelled. “You threatened my life, (expletive, expletive). (Expletive) you, you (expletive, expletive).” Foxman explained that he was tacking on the 10-day contempt of court sentence for a variety of reasons, including one particular McCarty outburst during the trial. “You also offered me to perform a sex act upon you, which I politely declined and still do to this day for whatever that’s worth,” Foxman said. The extra 10 days will be served when the 20-year sentence is done, and it's no small thing, explains the News Journal. It means that McCarty won't be able to participate in a work-release program while in prison. McCarty also was convicted of threatening 911 dispatchers, reports GateHouse Media Florida, which notes that because of his courtroom behavior, McCarty had to observe most of his trial from behind one-way glass.

final_summary:
In Today, Md., a series of deadly shootings shook Montgomery County, Maryland, resulting in the deaths of three individuals and injuring three others. The incidents unfolded over several locations, including the Westfield Montgomery Mall in Bethesda and a Giant grocery store in Aspen Hill. The suspect, identified as 62-year-old Eulalio Tordil, a federal law enforcement officer employed by the Federal Protective Service, was eventually apprehended without incident in the Aspen Hill area.

According to reports, the first shooting occurred on Thursday evening outside High Point High School in Beltsville, Prince George's County, where Tordil's estranged wife, Gladys Tordil, was fatally shot. A passerby who attempted to intervene was also injured. Tordil was subsequently added to the list of suspects, and authorities launched a manhunt.

On Friday morning, a second shooting took place at the Westfield Montgomery Mall in Bethesda, where one individual was killed and two others were wounded. Approximately an hour later, a third shooting occurred at a Giant grocery store in Aspen Hill, resulting in the death of a woman. 

As police investigated the incidents, they discovered that Tordil was still in the vicinity of the Aspen Hill area. Due to concerns that he may be armed, authorities opted to wait and observe him, rather than making an immediate arrest. Tordil was spotted entering various businesses, including a Boston Market, where he ate lunch, and a Michaels craft store, where he browsed. 

After approximately an hour of surveillance, Tordil entered his vehicle, prompting police to block the vehicle and apprehend him. A weapon was found in the vehicle. Tordil was taken into custody without incident and is currently being held at Montgomery County police headquarters.

Tordil's employment with the Federal Protective Service was terminated in March due to a protective order issued against him by a Prince George's County district judge. The order prohibited him from contacting his estranged wife and two minor children, citing allegations of physical and emotional abuse. Tordil was also required to surrender his duty weapon, badge, and credentials.

Prince George's County police have charged Tordil with first-degree murder in the shooting of his wife and assault for allegedly shooting a man who intervened. Montgomery County police have not announced any charges against Tordil. He is scheduled to appear in court on Monday in Rockville District Court.

The arrest of Tordil brings an end to a chaotic 24-hour period that saw multiple shootings and a massive manhunt. The incidents served as a stark reminder of the fear and terror caused by gun violence, echoing the sentiments expressed by Prince George's County Executive Rushern L. Baker III, who compared the situation to the 2002 Beltway sniper attacks that resulted in the deaths of 10 people.

reference: – Two people are dead following shootings at a shopping mall and grocery store Friday in Maryland, and police have a possible suspect in custody. The first shooting took place around 11am outside the Westfield Montgomery Mall in Bethesda, with two men and one woman hit, Fox 5 DC reports. Two of the victims were reportedly shot when attempting to help the first victim, and all three were hospitalized. According to ABC News, one of the men has died and another victim is in critical condition. Police say there is no indication the shooter knew the victims, the AP reports. The second shooting took place at a grocery store about eight miles from the mall and left a woman dead, the Washington Post reports. It's unclear if the two shootings are related. Police say Eulalio Tordil, wanted on suspicion of shooting and killing his estranged wife outside a Maryland high school on Thursday, may be responsible for one or both of Friday's shootings. He was arrested Friday afternoon, according to NBC News. Tordil allegedly followed his wife to the school, where she was picking up her children, and shot her. Police say he also shot and injured a person attempting to help his wife. CNN reports the 62-year-old Tordil is a law enforcement officer with the Federal Protective Service who was put on administrative leave in March after a protective order was issued against him.

final_summary:
A recent wave of misinformation has swept through social media platforms, particularly Twitter and Reddit, claiming that the latest iOS 8 software upgrade allows for wireless charging via microwave ovens. However, this claim has been thoroughly debunked as a hoax originating from a 4chan thread. According to reports, users who fell prey to the false information attempted to charge their iPhones in the microwave, resulting in damaged devices and potentially hazardous situations, just days after the release of iOS 8 on Last Wednesday.

The "Wave" hoax, as it has come to be known, is not an isolated incident. Similar pranks have circulated online in the past, including a previous attempt to deceive Apple users into believing that iOS 7 would render their phones waterproof, a notion that was also debunked last year. This latest instance highlights the persistence of such misinformation campaigns, often fueled by anonymous online communities like 4chan.

Twitter users, who had initially spread the false information, later took to the platform to share photos of damaged iPhones and microwaves, sparking concerns over the potential harm caused by these actions. Fortunately, no serious injuries have been reported. In response to the situation, Twitter reminded its users that they can opt out of sharing their location data, which may have inadvertently contributed to the spread of the hoax, and that when you tweet with a location, Twitter stores that location. You can switch location on/off before each Tweet and always have the option to delete your location history. Learn more.

The origins of the "Wave" hoax can be traced back to a 4chan thread, where users shared the false information. The platform has a history of hosting similar pranks, including a previous attempt to deceive Apple users about the water-resistance capabilities of iOS 7. Online communities like Reddit and YouTube have also played a role in perpetuating the hoax, with some videos promoting the fake feature having been uploaded to the video-sharing site for years. It remains unclear how many individuals fell victim to the hoax, but the incident serves as a reminder of the importance of verifying information before sharing it online.

reference: – We're going to say what should be unnecessary: You cannot charge your iPhone in the microwave. Or, as victims of the latest scam from the darlings at 4Chan have found, you can try, but the results won't be much better than sticking it in your pocket and sitting on it. The hoax is billed as "Wave," reports Mashable, an apparent "iOS8 exclusive" in which you purportedly charge your phone "wirelessly through microwave frequencies ... using any standard household microwave." The slick-looking ad for Wave, which has been making the rounds on Twitter and Reddit, helpfully recommends "60 seconds at 700W or 70 seconds at 800W." It's completely unknown how many suckers fell for Wave, but Mashable notes that "photos of fried iPhones and burned microwaves" are circulating. And apparently enough of them that the LAPD got in on the action, reports the LA Times. "This #Wave capability is a #hoax. Don't be fooled into microwaving your #iPhone6. #Apple #Smartphone," they tweeted. Meanwhile, angry Apple users with crispy iPhones aren't the only ones torqued at 4Chan: Celebrities are apparently firing back, too.

final_summary:
In a shocking turn of events, Iraq's military, trained and equipped by the U.S. and coalition forces, has been overrun by insurgent forces in several cities, including Mosul and Tikrit, in the Middle East, leading to widespread concern and calls for international intervention in Iraq this week. According to U.S. officials, the Iraqi military surrendered without a fight, highlighting a series of failures that allowed the Islamist fighters to gain control.

One factor contributing to the collapse was the lack of effective intelligence gathering, despite the U.S. having flown unmanned surveillance aircraft over Iraq since last year to monitor insurgent movements. However, the program proved insufficient in anticipating the swift takeover of Mosul and Tikrit by the Islamic State in Iraq and the Levant (ISIS). 

As the situation spirals out of control, U.S. President Barack Obama has announced that his administration is considering all options, including military action, to aid Iraq in its fight against ISIS. While the White House has ruled out deploying ground troops, Obama hinted at the possibility of air strikes, stating that the U.S. cannot allow ISIS to establish a permanent foothold in Iraq or Syria.

The crisis has deepened fears of a wider Sunni uprising, with reports emerging that former Baath Party members loyal to Saddam Hussein have joined forces with ISIS. The United Nations Security Council has condemned the violence and expressed support for Iraq's government and people in their struggle against terrorism. 

Meanwhile, the humanitarian situation in Mosul remains dire, with up to 500,000 people fleeing the city. The Kurdish forces have taken advantage of the chaos to claim control of the oil-rich city of Kirkuk, which they have long sought to incorporate into their autonomous region.

As the situation continues to deteriorate, the BBC's analysis suggests that ISIS's success could lead to a breakdown in regional stability, exacerbating the sectarian conflict between Sunnis and Shiites. Iran, a majority Shia Muslim country, may intervene directly or through its proxies, potentially inflaming tensions further.

With the Iraqi parliament unable to convene a quorum to grant Prime Minister Nouri Maliki emergency powers, the country teeters on the brink of collapse. The U.S., Australia, and other nations are weighing their responses, while the world watches anxiously as the situation unfolds. Reports from AFP indicate that several hundred defense contractors working with the Iraqi military are being temporarily relocated to safer areas, including Baghdad.

reference: – The US rebuffed Iraq's request for airstrikes as insurgents gained strength, but American drones were already collecting intelligence on the militants. The secret drone program, carried out with the consent of the Iraqi government since last year, failed to predict the rapid advance of militants who have now seized several cities, officials tell the Wall Street Journal. "It's not like it did any good," one US official says. President Obama says "all the options" are being considered to help Iraq and while the use of ground troops has been ruled out, officials are considering expanding the drone program. The al-Qaeda-linked Islamic State of Iraq and Syria militants have vowed to take the fight to Baghdad, and although the capital is not expected to fall, Americans in Iraq are keeping an eye on the exit. Officials say they have stepped up plans for the evacuation of the US Embassy in Baghdad, and the BBC reports that American defense contractors working with the Iraqi military are being shifted to safer areas. Hundreds of contractors "are being temporarily relocated by their companies due to security concerns in the area," according to a State Department spokeswoman.

final_summary:
In a heart-wrenching appeal, 14-year-old Valentina Maureira, a Chilean girl suffering from cystic fibrosis, released a video on her Facebook page on Sunday, pleading with President Michelle Bachelet to grant her permission to end her life through euthanasia. The video, which quickly went viral, showed Valentina speaking from her hospital bed, expressing her desire to be allowed to receive an injection that would put her to eternal rest due to her unbearable suffering from the genetic condition.

Valentina's family has been deeply affected by the disease, as her older brother also succumbed to cystic fibrosis. Her father, Freddy Maureira, revealed that his daughter has undergone five surgeries, which only exacerbated her pain and discomfort. Despite promises of improvement, Valentina's condition worsened, leaving her with constant vomiting and headaches. Speaking to local Radio Bio Bio, Mr. Maureira expressed his surprise at his daughter's decision to release the video.

The Universidad Catolica clinic in Santiago confirmed that Valentina is indeed a patient there, stating that she is currently stable, albeit with no immediate life-threatening conditions. However, the hospital did not provide further information on her specific situation. By Thursday, government officials had responded to Valentina's plea, with Spokesman Alvaro Elizalde ruling out euthanasia as an option, citing the existing laws in Chile, where euthanasia remains prohibited. Elizalde emphasized that the health ministry is working closely with the Maureira family to ensure Valentina receives necessary psychological and medical support.

President Bachelet, who has been focused on introducing reforms, including a bill to relax the abortion law, has not publicly addressed the issue of euthanasia. Nevertheless, her visit to Valentina on Saturday sparked a national debate on the topic, highlighting the complexities surrounding end-of-life care in Chile. As the nation grapples with this sensitive issue, Valentina's courageous plea serves as a poignant reminder of the human cost of restrictive laws and the need for compassionate solutions.

reference: – Valentina Maureira's brother died from cystic fibrosis when he was 6. The 14-year-old has the same inherited disease and was told she has a life expectancy of 17, reports CNN. Except Valentina doesn't want to live that long. The Chilean teen posted a now-viral video to Facebook late last month, a plea to President Michelle Bachelet that she be allowed to end her life. "I am tired of living with this sickness," which causes damage to the lungs and digestive system, Valentina explains. "Please authorize an injection so I can sleep forever." But a rep for Bachelet on Thursday explained "it's impossible to grant her wish" due to Chilean law, which bars euthanasia and doesn't allow a government official to circumvent that. Still, the president herself, who is also a pediatrician, did visit Valentina for more than an hour last week; the two posed for a selfie. The president's rep explained "we will provide all the emotional and psychological support and medical treatment to improve her living conditions." As for her medical condition, the Universidad Catolica clinic in Santiago describes it as stable and notes she is not facing, as Reuters puts it, any "immediately life-threatening conditions." But her father described a painful existence: "She has already had five operations ... which have caused her a lot of suffering. It was promised that things would get better, but for her it was worse." She suffers from persistent headaches and vomiting as well. In an interview with the AP, he says he "cried through the night" after learning what Valentina wanted, "but I have to respect her decision because she's the one who's suffering this illness." (One country has made euthanasia legal for kids.)

final_summary:
Oklahoma City, Oklahoma - A contentious dispute has erupted over the legitimacy of Representative Frank D. Lucas, a long-time incumbent who secured 82.8% of the vote in the recent Republican primary election held on Tuesday. Challenger Tim Murray, who garnered 5.2% of the vote, has chosen to contest the outcome, alleging that Lucas is deceased and has been replaced by a body double. According to Murray's campaign website, Lucas was supposedly executed by the World Court in Ukraine on January 11, 2011, and has since been impersonated by a lookalike. 

Murray's claims have sparked widespread ridicule, with Keith Gaddie, a University of Oklahoma professor, describing them as "the stupidest thing I've ever heard in Oklahoma politics." However, Murray remains resolute, reiterating his stance through a press release addressed to "News Person," stating that Lucas' alleged replacement is a "look alike" who has been masquerading as the congressman. 

Lucas himself has dismissed the allegations, confirming that he is indeed alive and denying any involvement with Ukraine. His office has declined further comment on the matter. The Oklahoma State Election Board has given Murray until 5 pm to file a formal petition to challenge the election results, although the deadline for contesting a candidate's qualifications had already passed on April 15. 

Murray's assertions bear some resemblance to the plot of the 1993 film "Dave," where a lookalike assumes the role of the US President, played by Kevin Kline. Despite the absurdity of his claims, Murray maintains that he will not employ artificial intelligence or robots to represent him in office, distinguishing himself from other Republican challengers. As the situation unfolds, it remains to be seen whether Murray will proceed with his challenge or accept the outcome of the election.

reference: – Frank Lucas only looks like he won an Oklahoma congressional primary by a landslide, according to a contender who got thumped at the polls. Tim Murray says he'll contest the primary because Lucas was actually hanged in Ukraine and replaced with a body double, THV 11 reports. "It is widely known" that Lucas "is no longer alive and has been displayed by a look alike," Murray writes on his website. "Rep. Lucas’ look alike was depicted as sentenced on a white stage in southern Ukraine on or about Jan. 11, 2011." But he doesn't stop there: "I am contesting that this matter has happen [sic] since his election was blocked, because of the US Defense Department’s use of Mr. Murray’s DNA. ... Congress is likely wanting me to state that all my DNA used will not result in benefits to people I have never had relations with of a family nature." Murray's reaction? "It does come as kind of a shock to read that you’re not you," he told KFOR. "I’ve never been to Ukraine." The GOP congressman even had a rep say that he's not dead and hasn't been to Ukraine, the Washington Post reports. At least one analyst isn't too surprised: "A lot of stupid things happen in Oklahoma politics," said Keith Gaddie of the University of Oklahoma. "But this may be the stupidest I've ever heard. Welcome to Oklahoma." Murray—who won 5.2% of the vote to Lucas' 82.8%—has until today to officially contest the primary.

final_summary:
Actress America Ferrera kicked off the countdown to 2018 with a festive message, wishing fans "Más Besos" - more kisses - as the clock struck midnight on December 31. This heartfelt greeting hinted at new beginnings and love in the coming year. Unbeknownst to Ferrera, one of Hollywood's most beloved stars, John Stamos, was indeed embarking on a new chapter in his personal life. In a surprise announcement to People magazine, Stamos revealed that he and his fiancée, actress Caitlin McHugh, were expecting their first child together, with Stamos set to become a father at the age of 54.

This milestone marked a significant moment for Stamos, who rose to fame playing the lovable Uncle Jesse on the popular 1990s sitcom "Full House." Joining an elite group of celebrities who have become fathers in their 50s, Stamos follows in the footsteps of George Clooney, who welcomed twins at the age of 56 with his wife Amal. Other notable celebrities, such as Alec Baldwin, Steve Martin, Clint Eastwood, Larry King, Robert De Niro, Rod Stewart, Hugh Hefner, and David Letterman, have also expanded their families in their later years.

Stamos' journey to parenthood began when he and McHugh started discussing starting a family. As he explained to People, "We have the same morals and the same values, that all clicked nicely. So we said, 'Oh, well, maybe we should have a family.'" McHugh playfully joked that Stamos was getting older and should consider having a child before tying the knot, to which he replied, "Why?" Their engagement, which took place at Disneyland, seemed to seal their fate as a family unit.

In an interview with People, Stamos expressed his excitement about becoming a father, stating, "I'll be a fun dad. I've been practicing for a long time." His fiancée, McHugh, praised Stamos for his kind and generous nature, saying, "He's always been wonderful with kids, and I'm sure he will be an amazing father." When asked if they plan to expand their family further, Stamos hinted that they might have more children in the future, with McHugh sharing similar sentiments.

As the couple prepares to welcome their newborn, fans and friends alike are eagerly awaiting the arrival of Stamos' first child. With his charming personality and dedication to his craft, it's no wonder that John Stamos is ready to take on the challenges and joys of fatherhood.
