reference: – The fired nanny who has been refusing to leave her former employers' home says she is now ready to go—when conditions are just right. Diane Stretton over the weekend emailed Marcella and Ralph Bracamonte's lawyer blaming the weather and the media for her failure to depart their California home, ABC News reports. There are "always a bunch of news vehicles right in front of the house," the 64-year-old complains. "The media needs to be completely gone before I continue moving." She noted a predicted near-100-degree heat wave will make it impossible to move in the next few days because she "can't work in that kind of heat"—though "if the media stays away, I will be out by the 4th of July. But that depends on the circus not continuing." But Marcella Bracamonte, who says she fired Stretton last month after she "just stopped working," isn't convinced. "I don’t believe her. She is going to show up when I am not here with a bunch of food and water and she will barricade herself in her room," she says. Stretton, for her part, tells KNX 1070 news radio she only refused to work twice, when she had the flu and had worked 90 days straight. "I didn’t get lunch breaks, I didn’t get coffee breaks, I didn't get any holidays. Basically, I was working 24/7." Bracamonte, who tells People that she hired Stretton through a Craigslist ad and that her references checked out, says she and her family will be out of town on the day when Stretton claims she is going to move—but there will be plenty of other family members house-sitting. "I feel like she knows that I'm going to be gone and that she wants to lock me out of my home," she says.

final_summary:
A recent study conducted by the Chronicle of Higher Education reveals a concerning trend in the nation's higher education system. According to the report, a staggering 33% of four-year colleges and universities experienced a decline in graduation rates between 2002 and 2008. This decline is attributed to various factors, including the economic downturn, which led to students taking longer than six years to complete their degrees.

The study analyzed data from over 1,000 public and private non-profit institutions, focusing on research, master's, and baccalaureate colleges. The data shows that while some institutions have made significant strides in improving their graduation rates, others have struggled to maintain their performance. For instance, the University of Virginia saw a notable increase in graduation rates, rising from 93% in 2002 to 94% in 2008. Similarly, the University of Rochester improved its graduation rate from 81% in 2002 to 84% in 2008.

On the other hand, some institutions have seen a significant decline in graduation rates. The University of Tulsa, for example, dropped from 63% in 2002 to 63% in 2008. The University of Southern California also saw a notable improvement, increasing its graduation rate from 76% in 2002 to 88% in 2008.

The study highlights the importance of considering the impact of selectivity on graduation rates. Institutions that became more selective during the period covered by the study tended to experience an increase in graduation rates. Conversely, those that became less selective saw a decline in their graduation rates. For instance, the University of Notre Dame remained highly selective throughout the period, maintaining a graduation rate of 96% in 2008. In contrast, the University of San Francisco became less selective, resulting in a slight decline in its graduation rate from 67% in 2002 to 65% in 2008.

Other notable trends observed in the study include the significant improvements made by several institutions. The University of California-Berkeley also saw a notable improvement, rising from 74% in 2002 to 81% in 2008. The University of Pennsylvania maintained a graduation rate of 95% throughout the period.

However, the study also highlights the challenges faced by some institutions. The University of Virginia experienced a slight decline in graduation rates, dropping from 93% in 2002 to 93% in 2008. 

Overall, the study provides valuable insights into the complex issues facing the nation's higher education system. While some institutions have made significant progress in improving graduation rates, others continue to struggle. As policymakers and educators work to address these challenges, it is essential to consider the impact of selectivity, demographic changes, and other factors on graduation rates. By doing so, they can develop targeted strategies to support students and help them achieve their academic goals.

The Internet Archive has been working towards archiving web pages to preserve content and prevent broken links. As part of this initiative, they are crawling websites, including those listed under the "No More 404" category. Meanwhile, a unique opportunity has arisen for individuals seeking a tranquil lifestyle amidst felines. God's Little People Cat Rescue, based on the picturesque Greek Island of **The Greek Island Of Syros**, located in the Aegean Sea to the southeast of Athens, Greece, is hiring a live-in caretaker. The position comes with complimentary housing and a salary, as advertised on the organization's Facebook page, which notes that this is a genuine job offer and not a joke.

For those interested in animal welfare, another notable entity is Resqcats, a non-profit cat sanctuary situated in Santa Barbara, California. Founded by Jeffyne Telson, Resqcats dedicates itself to rescuing, caring for, and rehoming abandoned cats and kittens. According to Telson, many of the rescued animals require personalized attention to ensure they find suitable forever homes, taking into account factors such as compatibility with young children or other pets. Visitors can explore the online sanctuary, known as the **Online Resqcats Sanctuary**, to view adoptable cats and kittens. The website also features a section on resources, news, and newsletters, providing valuable information for potential adopters and supporters. Interestingly, the website includes a quote from artist Alan Wnuk, highlighting the importance of self-perception. In addition to its mission, Resqcats' daily activities are highlighted in **The Daily Hustle**. Despite the differing settings, both God's Little People Cat Rescue and Resqcats share a common goal – to provide loving homes for cats in need.

reference: – When Joan and Richard Bowell decided it was time to leave the Greek island of Syros, they first needed to find someone to manage the cat sanctuary they'd established there. God's Little People Cat Rescue, which started with one stray mama cat and her two kittens, expanded to more than 60 cats roaming the Bowells' property as they continued to take in strays. They posted a Facebook ad for a modestly paid job managing the sanctuary—and 40,000 people responded. The ad went viral, spawning headlines about the "dream job" or "purrrfect job," leading the Bowells to explain to would-be caretakers that it would, in fact, often be a difficult job—not only would poop have to be scooped and the like, but "heartbreaking decisions" would need to be made about sick cats. Months later, as the Washington Post reports, the sanctuary's caretaker has been chosen. Jeffyne Telson's husband sent her the ad soon after it was posted in August, the 62-year-old recalls. Telson runs her own cat rescue in Santa Barbara, Calif., only taking in strays; she's adopted out 3,000 cats and kittens over the past 21 years and has kept those who can't be placed at her own small sanctuary. Her application stood out, and the Bowells traveled to California to visit the couple in September. Joan and Jeffyne clicked immediately, and the Bowells offered Telson the job. She'll run the Syros sanctuary for some months, while volunteers run her Santa Barbara rescue, and other finalists for the Syros job will likely then take their own turns managing God's Little People. Meanwhile, the Bowells are looking into expanding the sanctuary, possibly even using it as an international training center for similarly-minded volunteers.

final_summary:
On August 12, 2017, in Charlottesville, Virginia, a white nationalist rally turned deadly as a car plowed into a crowd of counter-protesters, resulting in the deaths of three individuals. The incident occurred amidst a day of violent clashes between white supremacists and anti-fascist groups, with hundreds of people facing off in the city's downtown area. The rally, organized by right-wing blogger Jason Kessler, aimed to protest the city's decision to remove a statue of Confederate General Robert E. Lee from a downtown park.

As tensions escalated, a 20-year-old man from Ohio, identified as James Alex Fields Jr., allegedly drove a silver Dodge Charger into a group of people on the Downtown Mall, striking 32-year-old Heather Heyer, who later succumbed to her injuries. Fields was taken into custody after a brief chase and is being held without bond at the Albemarle-Charlottesville Regional Jail on charges of second-degree murder and malicious wounding.

In addition to the fatal car attack, two Virginia State Police troopers, Lieutenant H. Jay Cullen and Trooper-pilot Berke M.M. Bates, died in a helicopter crash while assisting law enforcement efforts. The incident occurred just hours after the car plowed into the crowd, and officials initially reported that the crash was linked to the rally.

White nationalist leader Richard Spencer, who was scheduled to speak at the rally, denied responsibility for the violence and blamed state and local police for the chaos. However, he acknowledged that he was pepper-sprayed twice during the day and encouraged attendees to disperse after the state of emergency was declared.

President Donald Trump condemned the violence, tweeting that "there is no place for this kind of violence in America" and urging unity. First Lady Melania Trump also called for peace, stating that "our country encourages freedom of speech, but let's communicate without hate in our hearts."

The Associated Press reported that the National Hockey League's Detroit Red Wings distanced themselves from a Michigan-based white nationalist group using their logo, stating they "vehemently disagree" with the event and are exploring legal action.

A civil rights investigation into the deadly car attack has been launched by the Richmond FBI Office, the Civil Rights Division, and the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Western District of Virginia. The investigation aims to gather all available facts and evidence surrounding the incident.

Three additional arrests were made in connection to the violent clashes, including a 21-year-old man from Tennessee, a 21-year-old man from Virginia, and a 44-year-old man from Florida, who were charged with disorderly conduct, assault and battery, and carrying a concealed handgun, respectively.

reference: – US officials have opened a civil rights investigation into the deadly car attack that took place amid clashes of white nationalists and counter-demonstrators, reports the AP. The investigation was announced late Saturday by the US Attorney's Office for the Western District of Virginia and the FBI's Richmond field office. Attorney General Jeff Sessions says US Attorney Rick Mountcastle has begun the investigation and will have the full support of the Justice Department. Sessions says, "The violence and deaths in Charlottesville strike at the heart of American law and justice. When such actions arise from racial bigotry and hatred, they betray our core values and cannot be tolerated." In other developments: The man who allegedly plowed his car into a crowd of protesters, James Alex Fields Jr., a 20-year-old from Ohio, was charged with second-degree murder and three counts of malicious wounding, reports NBC 29. Troy Dunigan, a 21-year-old from Chattanooga, Tenn., was charged with disorderly conduct; Jacob L. Smith, a 21-year-old from Louisa, Va., was charged with assault and battery; and James M. O'Brien, 44, of Gainesville, Fla., was charged with carrying a concealed handgun. White nationalist Richard Spencer accused police of endangering lives in how they handled the rally, saying he "did not attempt to engage in any kind of violence. So the idea that I could be held responsible is absurd. It's like blaming the fire department for a fire." Spencer also said he found President Trump's comments on the violence to be "rather vague and kind of lame." Fields' mother says he told her he was going to the rally and that she told him "to be careful" and peaceful. Rally organizer Jason Kessler said whoever drove a car into counter-protesters "did the wrong thing." He said he was saddened people were hurt and criticized law enforcement's response, saying they did a poor job controlling the chaos to allow free speech.

final_summary:
As the conflict in Libya continues to escalate on Friday, regional organizations and international leaders are emphasizing the need for a swift and inclusive transition to democracy. In a meeting in New York on Friday, UN Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon discussed the crisis with representatives from the League of Arab States, the Organization of Islamic Cooperation, the African Union, and the European Union, all agreeing that the crisis has entered a critical phase and that a smooth transition is crucial. The transition must be based on inclusivity, reconciliation, and national unity, with a focus on ending the conflict, restoring order, and establishing stability.

In Tripoli, the capital city, fighting persisted on Friday, with widespread shortages of fuel, food, and medical supplies affecting millions of people. The international community has pledged to provide assistance, with the UN releasing $1.5 billion in frozen Libyan assets to address immediate humanitarian needs. However, more needs to be done to ensure a stable transition of power, according to Ban Ki-moon.

Despite the ongoing fighting, Muammar Gaddafi's grip on power appears to be weakening, with opposition forces making significant gains. Principal Deputy Press Secretary Josh Earnest stated that the rebels have momentum on their side, having advanced and established a new government in Libya. Gaddafi's whereabouts remain unknown, but Earnest called on him to relinquish power and face accountability.

On Friday, rebel forces took control of various areas, including the former US airbase in Tripoli, which was converted into a military airport by Gaddafi. The base showcases the decay of Gaddafi's military, with long lines of helicopters and jets rusting on the tarmac. In contrast, the opposition has made significant progress, with the National Transitional Council (NTC) announcing its relocation from Benghazi to Tripoli on Thursday.

However, the conflict has resulted in numerous human rights abuses, with reports emerging of summary killings and torture by both rebel and loyalist forces. The UN has condemned these actions, urging all parties to prevent further violence and revenge attacks. The NTC's chairman, Mustafa Abdul Jalil, has warned rebels against engaging in revenge attacks, threatening to resign if his warning is ignored.

Meanwhile, the African Union has yet to recognize the NTC as Libya's legitimate representative, with South Africa stalling the release of $1.5 billion in Libyan assets due to concerns about officially acknowledging the NTC. The UN has agreed to modify the motion to remove the reference to the NTC, paving the way for the asset release.

In Sirte, Gaddafi's hometown, rebels are facing fierce resistance, with reports of NATO airstrikes and heavy fighting on Thursday. The city remains a strategic location, with Gaddafi pouring resources into its development. The NTC has announced plans to move its political base to Tripoli, with half of the leadership already relocated. However, the process of restoring essential services and supplies is on hold until Gaddafi is killed or captured.

In Abu Salim, a district in Tripoli, fighting has been intense on Thursday, with rebels battling Gaddafi loyalists in a bid to take control of the area. The district has seen some of the heaviest fighting this week, with reports of sniper fire and explosions. The area has been contested for several days, with rebels struggling to gain a foothold.

The situation in Libya remains dire, with widespread human suffering and a lack of basic necessities. The international community must work together to address the crisis, providing aid and support to the Libyan people. The fate of Gaddafi remains uncertain, but one thing is clear: the people of Libya deserve a peaceful and inclusive transition to democracy.

In a meeting in New York, UN Secretary-General Ban Ki-moon discussed the crisis with representatives from the League of Arab States, the Organization of Islamic Cooperation, the African Union, and the European Union, all agreeing that the crisis has entered a critical phase and that a smooth transition is crucial. The transition must be based on inclusivity, reconciliation, and national unity, with a focus on ending the conflict, restoring order, and establishing stability.

In Tripoli, the capital city, fighting persists, with widespread shortages of fuel, food, and medical supplies affecting millions of people. The international community has pledged to provide assistance, with the UN releasing $1.5 billion in frozen Libyan assets to address immediate humanitarian needs. However, more needs to be done to ensure a stable transition of power, according to Ban Ki-moon.

Despite the ongoing fighting, Moammar Gadhafi's grip on power appears to be weakening, with opposition forces making significant gains. Principal Deputy Press Secretary Josh Earnest stated that the rebels have momentum on their side, having advanced and established a new government in Libya. Gadhafi's whereabouts remain unknown, but Earnest called on him to relinquish power and face accountability.

Rebel forces have taken control of various areas, including the former US airbase in Tripoli, which was converted into a military airport by Gadhafi. The base showcases the decay of Gadhafi's military, with long lines of helicopters and jets rusting on the tarmac. The money had been seized by the US in the spring. It won’t go straight to the rebels, notes the New York Times: Instead, the US and international agencies will direct it toward humanitarian efforts. The head of the Transitional National Council pledged to “favor the countries which helped us” in accordance with “the support which they gave us.” With about half the Council in Tripoli, the city is largely in rebel hands—but fighting has continued, including reports of summary killings by both rebels and loyalists. Meanwhile, rebels have met opposition in their struggle to reach Moammar Gadhafi’s hometown, Sirte, which British planes bombed last night, hammering what Britain called a “large headquarters bunker,” CNN reports. final_summary:
ABC News reports that the federal government plans to order emergency inspections on 175 Boeing 737 airliners, and is rethinking its approach to plane inspections after a Southwest Airlines jet tore open in mid-flight on Friday night. 

Inspections will initially focus on 175 planes, used by airlines around the world, that make frequent takeoffs and landings. Eighty of the planes are in service in the United States, most of them for Southwest Airlines. The government is particularly concerned about older 737-300, 737-400, and 737-500 jets that have taken off and landed more than 30,000 times. Jets that have accumulated many flight cycles are apparently more likely to develop the sort of fatigue cracks that may have caused the tear in the skin of the Southwest 737-300 last week.

As the nation's planes age, more jets could cause concern and require inspection for such fatigue cracks. Inspectors use something called eddy current technology, passing an electric current through an aircraft's skin to look for small cracks. If any warning signs are detected, more sophisticated ultrasound and X-ray tools are then used for a closer examination. In some areas, a plane's skin can be as thin as a nickel.

Fatigue Cracks Found in Three Other Southwest Jets 
Southwest said today that it has inspected some 90 percent of its 737-300s, which were removed from service after the weekend. So far, inspections have uncovered fatigue cracks in three planes, the airline said. 64 other planes have been inspected and returned to service, with a dozen other planes still requiring inspection. The airline believes its inspection process complies with the government's order.

Due to the voluntary grounding, Southwest has had to cancel at least 600 flights since Friday's emergency landing. Southwest flight 812, en route from Phoenix to Sacramento, Calif., was diverted to a military base at Yuma, Ariz., after a section of the plane's fuselage ripped open, depressurizing the plane and exposing the sky to passengers.

On Friday's harrowing flight, the first 20 minutes all appeared normal as the plane climbed to 36,000 feet. Flight attendants had just taken drink orders when the plane's 118 passengers reported hearing loud pops. Then, with the plane at approximately 34,000 feet, the roof opened up near the luggage compartment and oxygen masks dropped in front of passengers. At least one flight attendant passed out.

Passenger Wade Allemand said he almost passed out. "Your ears instantly start to hurt really bad. You feel like you're going to black out," he said. The crew turned the jet sharply to the right, pushed the nose down, losing more than 20,000 feet in just four and a half minutes. Passengers had a short amount of time to get their oxygen masks on.

"It depends on what the decompression is. Sometimes it's minutes, sometimes it's two to three seconds," Lauren Jarmoszko, a flight safety instructor, said. Loss of oxygen impacts cognition, memory, and reaction time. It can also lead to brain damage. In a rapid decompression, like the Southwest flight, the oxygen mask drops quickly. There are also instances of slow decompression which is usually caused by a small leak in a window or door seal. Passengers and crew may not even notice the first effects: fatigue, mental confusion, and dulled reflexes.

The captain on Flight 812 had 19 years experience with Southwest, while his first officer had seven years of experience, and he received assistance as typical when declaring a emergency, according to the NTSB. Some terrified passengers clearly thought it was the end. One woman whose husband was on the plane received a text from him -- "Plane going down. Love you."

However, the plane was able to land at Yuma Marine Corps Air Station/International Airport at 4:07 p.m. Friday, said Ian Gregor of the Federal Aviation Administration. Passengers applauded the pilots upon landing, called loved ones and waited for a new Southwest jet to pick them up and take them on their way.

The incident has raised questions about the safety of aging aircraft, with some experts suggesting that the tear may have been caused by corrosion from moisture entering the joint between two thin bands of aluminum skin, exacerbated by the repeated expansion and contraction of the aircraft's skin during takeoff and landing cycles. The FAA's decision to inspect these aircraft using electromagnetic processes capable of detecting invisible cracks marks a significant shift in the agency's approach to ensuring the integrity of aging aircraft.

Southwest Airlines has already begun inspecting its fleet, with 64 of its 737-300s cleared for return to service, while 12 others remain pending inspection. The airline has canceled over 600 flights since the incident, and another Southwest flight was forced to make an emergency landing due to a burning electrical smell. The damaged aircraft, a 15-year-old 737-300, had undergone a comprehensive inspection just three years ago, but the incident highlights the need for more

In a shocking turn of events, a Southwest Airlines jet suffered a catastrophic failure on Friday when a 5-foot tear ripped open its roof during a routine flight from Phoenix to Sacramento, forcing an emergency landing at Yuma Marine Corps Air Station/International Airport. The incident has sent shockwaves throughout the aviation industry, prompting the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) to order emergency inspections of 80 older Boeing 737s, including 78 operated by Southwest Airlines, due to concerns over potentially hazardous cracks in the aircraft's skin.

According to the FAA, the inspections will target 737-300, 737-400, and 737-500 models built in the 1980s and 1990s, specifically those with more than 30,000 flights, which are deemed to be at higher risk of developing fatigue cracks. The move comes after National Transportation Safety Board (NTSB) investigators found additional cracks on the same seam in the skin where the tear occurred, and Southwest Airlines itself discovered similar issues on three additional 737-300s, leading to their grounding.

The incident has raised questions about the safety of aging aircraft, with some experts suggesting that the tear may have been caused by corrosion from moisture entering the joint between two thin bands of aluminum skin, exacerbated by the repeated expansion and contraction of the aircraft's skin during takeoff and landing cycles. The FAA's decision to inspect these aircraft using electromagnetic processes capable of detecting invisible cracks marks a significant shift in the agency's approach to ensuring the integrity of aging aircraft.

Southwest Airlines has already begun inspecting its fleet, with 64 of its 737-300s cleared for return to service, while 12 others remain pending inspection. The airline has canceled over 600 flights since the incident, and another Southwest flight was forced to make an emergency landing due to a burning electrical smell. The damaged aircraft, a 15-year-old 737-300, had undergone a comprehensive inspection just three years ago, but the incident highlights the need for more stringent checks on aging aircraft.

As the investigation continues, the FAA and Boeing are reevaluating their inspection protocols, with Boeing announcing plans to issue a bulletin to airlines recommending inspections. The incident has sparked concerns among aviation experts, who warn that the problem may be more widespread, affecting not only Southwest Airlines but also other carriers operating similar aircraft. With the FAA taking swift action to address the issue, the aviation community remains on high alert, awaiting the results of the investigation and potential changes to safety regulations.

final_summary:
On Tuesday, Keith Lamont Scott, a 43-year-old black man, was fatally shot by a police officer in Charlotte, North Carolina. The incident sparked widespread protests across the city, with demonstrators calling for the release of police video footage of the shooting. Following days of pressure, the Charlotte-Mecklenburg Police Department released partial footage of the incident on September 24, 2016.

According to the police, officers were attempting to serve an arrest warrant on an unrelated individual when they noticed Keith Scott rolling a marijuana "blunt" in his vehicle. However, upon further observation, they spotted a gun in the vehicle, which led them to withdraw and return in marked uniforms, identifying themselves as police officers. Despite repeated commands to drop the gun, Scott failed to comply, and Officer Brentley Vinson, who was not wearing a body camera, shot and killed Scott.

Justin Bamberg, a lawyer representing the Scott family, stated that the released footage does not clearly show Scott holding a gun. The released footage, captured by a police dashboard camera and a body-worn camera, shows Scott exiting his vehicle and backing away from the officers while his hands are at his sides. However, it is unclear from the footage whether Scott was holding a gun at the time of the shooting. The videos do not depict the actual shooting, and the officer who fired the fatal shots is not visible.

In a press conference, Police Chief Kerr Putney stated that the released footage supports the overall evidence in the case, including witness statements, forensic analysis, and interviews with officers at the scene. Putney maintained that Vinson acted appropriately in response to what appeared to be an imminent threat.

Rakeyia Scott, Keith's wife, had previously released a cellphone video of the encounter, which showed her pleading with officers not to shoot her husband, whom she described as unarmed and suffering from a traumatic brain injury. The video sparked widespread outrage and calls for the release of the police footage.

Governor Pat McCrory, who had declared a state of emergency in response to the protests, praised the decision to release the videos, stating that it would help to promote transparency while protecting the integrity of the investigation. US Representative Alma Adams expressed hope that the release would facilitate healing and dialogue within the community.

As the investigation into the shooting continues, the North Carolina State Bureau of Investigation is leading the inquiry, and the Charlotte-Mecklenburg Police Department has stated that they will not charge any officers involved in the incident. The case has reignited debates about police accountability, racial tensions, and the use of force in law enforcement.

In addition to the video, police also released a photo of the gun they say was spotted in Scott's vehicle. Some early descriptions: Charlotte Observer: The videos "do not show Keith Lamont Scott raising a weapon toward officers nor a gun in his hand." NBC News: "The dashcam video released shows Scott come out of a white SUV while police stand behind another vehicle with their weapons raised and command him to drop the gun. Scott eventually emerges from the SUV slowly and backs away. As he is backing up, four shots can be heard, and Scott can be seen falling to the ground." AP: "Scott can be seen in police dashboard camera video backing away from his SUV with his hands down, and it's unclear if there's anything in the man's hands. Four shots are heard, and he falls to the ground." ABC News: "The actual shooting is neither seen nor heard in the body cam footage.Officer Brentley Vinson, identified by police as the officer who shot Scott, cannot be seen firing his weapon in either video." Rakeyia Scott released her own video of her husband's shooting Friday.

final_summary:
A long-standing stereotype in the gaming community has been debunked by a recent study conducted by researchers at the University of California, Davis, led by Assistant Professor Cuihua "Cindy" Shen, who has been analyzing massively multiplayer online game data since 2007. The study aimed to investigate whether men are indeed better gamers than women, a notion that has persisted despite the growing presence of female gamers in the industry, with women now making up about 20 percent of players in multiplayer online games.

Shen and her team, which included colleagues from Michigan State University, The University of Southern California, and The University of Illinois, Urbana-Champaign, analyzed data from over 11,000 players across two massively multiplayer online games, "EverQuest II" in the United States and "Chevaliers' Romance III" in China. The researchers controlled for various factors that could influence gameplay, such as playing time, character choice, and guild membership, and found that once these variables were accounted for, the gender gap in performance disappeared.

According to Shen, the study's findings suggest that women advanced at least as fast as men in both games, contradicting the widespread stereotype that men are superior gamers. The study's results have significant implications for the gaming community, particularly in light of the ongoing struggle against sexism and harassment faced by female gamers, which was highlighted by the GamerGate controversy in 2014.

The prevalence of sexism in the gaming community was also noted by Shen, who cited the need to address the stereotype that women are inferior gamers, which could contribute to a self-fulfilling prophecy, deterring women from pursuing careers in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics. Shen suggested promoting inclusive gaming environments, such as the PMS Clan, a female-oriented gaming community that fosters a sense of belonging among its members. Additionally, game designers can create more welcoming spaces by implementing features like the Tribunal, a system that allows players to report and address harassment.

The study's findings were published in the Journal of Computer-Mediated Communication and provide valuable insights into the gaming community's attitudes towards women. The study's results offer a crucial step towards breaking down barriers and promoting greater inclusivity in the gaming industry.

The study aimed to investigate whether men are indeed better gamers than women, a notion that has persisted despite the growing presence of female gamers in the industry, with women now making up about 20 percent of players in multiplayer online games.

Shen and her team, which included colleagues from Michigan State University, The University of Southern California, and The University of Illinois, Urbana-Champaign, analyzed data from over 11,000 players across two massively multiplayer online games, "EverQuest II" in the United States and "Chevaliers' Romance III" in China. The researchers controlled for various factors that could influence gameplay, such as playing time, character choice, and guild membership, and found that once these variables were accounted for, the gender gap in performance disappeared.

According to Shen, the study's findings suggest that women advanced at least as fast as men in both games, contradicting the widespread stereotype that men are superior gamers. The study's results have significant implications for the gaming community, particularly in light of the ongoing struggle against sexism and harassment faced by female gamers, which was highlighted by the GamerGate controversy in 2014.

Shen suggested promoting inclusive gaming environments, such as the PMS Clan, a female-oriented gaming community that fosters a sense of belonging among its members. Leslie Gibson, a Republican candidate for Maine's 57th District House of Representatives seat, abruptly ended his campaign on Friday after facing intense backlash for his derogatory comments towards Parkland, Florida school shooting survivors Emma González and David Hogg. Gibson, who had initially been the sole declared candidate for the position, had taken to social media to criticize the teenagers, labeling González a "skinhead lesbian" and Hogg a "bald-faced liar." His comments sparked widespread outrage, with many condemning his behavior as unacceptable.

Gibson's tweets were in response to the growing influence of the Parkland survivors, particularly González and Hogg, who have become prominent advocates for stricter gun control measures in the aftermath of the mass shooting that claimed 17 lives at Marjory Stoneman Douglas High School. González, who was a student at the school, has become a vocal critic of President Trump's proposal to arm teachers and has led chants of "shame on you" directed at lawmakers who oppose stricter gun regulations.

Hogg, another student who survived the shooting, has also gained a significant following on social media and has been critical of the National Rifle Association (NRA), accusing its spokesperson, Dana Loesch, of working with gun manufacturers to control Congress. Gibson's comments were seen as a reflection of his strong stance in favor of the NRA and his opposition to gun control measures.

As the controversy surrounding Gibson's comments grew, several individuals stepped forward to challenge him in the election. Republican Thomas Martin Jr., a former state senator from Benton, and Democrat Eryn Gilchrist, a 28-year-old medical device company employee from the town of Sabattus, which is part of the 57th District, both filed paperwork to run for the seat. Gilchrist stated that she was motivated to run due to her horror and embarrassment at the prospect of Gibson representing her in the legislature, while Martin cited Gibson's comments as the reason for his decision to enter the race.

In a surprising turn of events, Gibson announced his withdrawal from the campaign on Friday, stating that it was "the best thing for everybody." He maintained that he was not leaving with his head hung low, but rather with his head held high. Following his announcement, González and fellow activist Sarah Chadwick expressed their approval of Gibson's decision, with González tweeting "Thank You" to Gilchrist, who will now face Martin in the election.

The controversy surrounding Gibson's comments highlights the increasingly polarized nature of American politics, particularly in the context of gun control debates. The incident serves as a reminder of the importance of respectful dialogue and civility in public discourse, even in the face of differing opinions.

reference: – A Maine candidate's attack on an outspoken survivor of the Parkland school shooting angered so many people that he ended up having to drop out of a race in which he had been running unopposed. Leslie Gibson, a Republican who had been running for a state House seat in the 57th district, tweeted that Emma Gonzalez was a "skinhead lesbian" with nothing to say "unless you’re a frothing at the mouth moonbat." In another tweet from his now-deleted account, Gibson rejected Gonzalez's claim to be a survivor of the mass shooting, saying she had been in a "completely different part of the school," BuzzFeed reports. He also called David Hogg, another survivor, a "bald-faced liar," prompting Hogg to ask whether anybody would run against him, the Washington Post reports. After the tweets—which were denounced by several Republican state lawmakers—28-year-old Democrat Eryn Gilchrist announced her candidacy, saying she would be "horrified and embarrassed" to have Gibson represent her. Thomas Martin Jr., a Republican, also announced his candidacy, saying he represented "real Republican values," the Portland Press Herald reports. Gibson, who apologized to Gonzalez earlier in the week for the "wrong and unacceptable" way in which he addressed her, dropped out of the race Friday, saying he had made the decision after speaking to family members, friends, and Martin. "I am not walking away with my head hung low. I am walking away with my head held high," he said. (After the Parkland shooting, Gonzalez called out President Trump and the NRA.)

final_summary:
Henri Bendel, a storied American luxury department store, has been a mainstay of high-end fashion for 123 years, but after a long history, the iconic retailer is set to close its doors permanently in January 2019, as announced by parent company L Brands on Thursday. According to a statement released by Ben Hider/Getty Images, the decision comes as a result of declining sales, with L Brands planning to close Bendel's e-commerce platform and all 23 physical locations, including its flagship store on Fifth Avenue, situated directly across from Bergdorf Goodman and Tiffany & Co.

According to Leslie Wexner, chairman and CEO of L Brands, the decision to shutter Bendel was made in order to improve company profitability and focus on larger brands with greater growth potential, such as Victoria's Secret and Bath & Body Works. Despite being a beloved institution, Bendel's sales of approximately $85 million represented only a small fraction of L Brands' overall revenue of $12.6 billion in 2018.

Founded in 1895 by Henri Bendel, the store initially operated as a millinery shop in Greenwich Village before moving to its iconic Fifth Avenue location in 1912. It was during this time that Bendel became one of the first luxury retailers to establish a presence on upper Fifth Avenue, a feat achieved in 1913 when it opened its flagship store at the corner of 5th Avenue and 57th Street. Over the years, Bendel played host to numerous notable designers, including Coco Chanel, whose work was introduced to the US market through the store in 1913, and Andy Warhol, who served as the store's in-house illustrator in the 1960s.

The closure of Bendel marks another blow to the struggling retail landscape, with L Brands facing significant challenges, including increased competition and a decline in stock value, which has plummeted by 55% in 2018, making it one of the worst-performing companies in the S&P 500 for the year. While the fate of the Fifth Avenue store remains uncertain beyond January 2019, it is clear that the closure of Bendel represents the end of an era in American fashion history.

final_summary:
In a bizarre incident that has left many stunned, a 36-year-old woman returned from her honeymoon in Belize with an unwelcome souvenir - a living creature embedded in her skin. Two months after her tropical getaway in Florida, the woman visited Tampa General Hospital complaining of an itchy skin lesion on her groin area, which she initially believed might be an insect bite. Her primary care physician prescribed her a course of antibiotics, but the lesion persisted even after completing the treatment in Florida.

Undeterred, the woman sought a second opinion at Memorial Hospital in Tampa, where she met Dr. Enrico Camporesi, a wound healing specialist. Camporesi was perplexed by the lesion, describing the surrounding skin as hard to the touch, similar to having an egg or bean underneath. He consulted a surgeon, who suspected that the hard bump might be a small creature lodged beneath the skin. The surgeon's intuition proved correct, and upon further examination, the team discovered a human botfly larva, also known as a maggot, embedded in the woman's skin.

According to Dr. Camporesi, the botfly larva had burrowed into the woman's skin, leaving behind a hole for breathing purposes. The larva, which resembled a small, tapered insect with rows of spines and hooks, was identified by pathologists as a human botfly larva, specifically Dermatobia hominis. This type of infestation is medically referred to as myiasis.

The botfly larva had likely entered the woman's skin through a mosquito or fly that had previously come into contact with the female botfly's eggs. These eggs are typically laid on other insects, which then transmit them to humans through bites. Once inside the human host, the larva feeds on tissue and grows, eventually emerging from the skin after 27 to 128 days.

This peculiar case highlights the risks associated with traveling to the tropics, including areas in Florida, where botflies are relatively common. While botfly infestations are rare in the United States, they are a significant concern in Central America, particularly in Belize. In fact, the woman's experience is not unique, as botfly infestations are among the top four most common skin diseases reported by travelers returning from developing countries, especially those who visit the tropics.

Fortunately, the woman received prompt medical attention and underwent surgery to remove the larva. Within a week of the procedure, her skin had completely healed. However, if left untreated, the larva could have continued to grow, potentially creating a larger hole in the skin as it matured into an adult fly.

Earlier this month, Dr. Camporesi's account of the incident was published in The Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports, shedding light on this unusual condition and the importance of being aware of potential health risks when traveling abroad. As reported by Live Science, this case serves as a reminder of the unexpected consequences of venturing into unfamiliar territories, where exotic creatures like the human botfly larva can pose a threat to unsuspecting tourists.

reference: – A tiny creature grew inside a Florida woman for two months after her honeymoon in Belize—and no, she wasn't pregnant. Weeks after returning home, the 36-year-old noticed an itchy spot on the left side of her groin and assumed she'd been bitten by an insect, reports Live Science. Thinking it was an infected spider bite, her doctor fruitlessly prescribed antibiotics. Dr. Enrico Camporesi of Tampa's Memorial Hospital next took a look, noticing a hole in the center of the wound, which felt hard, as though something were inside. A surgeon suspected it was a living thing, and turned out to be right. Cutting open the lesion, doctors found a tapered human botfly larva with rows of spines and hooks meant to keep it buried in the woman's skin for up to 128 days, reports the Miami Herald. Commonly found in Central America, a human botfly first deposits her eggs on an insect, like a mosquito, which carries them to human or animal hosts. Injected in skin via bites, the egg hatches into larva that eventually drops from the skin and continues developing, eventually reaching the size of a large bumblebee, per the Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports. Though the botfly didn't reach the pupa stage in this case, it wasn't necessary for its human host to go under the knife. Belizeans cover lesions—with petroleum jelly, nail polish, and even bacon strips, per the Herald—to suffocate larvae, which emerge for air and can be removed with tweezers. Still, the Florida patient was healed within a week. (Caterpillar larvae might prove useful.)

In the Peruvian Amazon, a tragic series of events unfolded, resulting in the lynching of a 41-year-old Canadian national, Sebastian Woodroffe, and the murder of an 81-year-old indigenous spiritual healer, Olivia Arévalo. Woodroffe, a resident of Vancouver Island, Canada, had traveled to the Ucayali region of the Peruvian Amazon to pursue an apprenticeship with a plant healer from the Shipibo indigenous group, with the aim of becoming an addiction counselor using hallucinogenic medicine. 

According to friends and acquaintances, Woodroffe had visited the region multiple times to experiment with ayahuasca, a hallucinogenic brew made from native plants, and had expressed a deep respect for the local culture and traditions. However, on Thursday, Arévalo, a respected spiritual healer and rights activist, was shot dead outside her home in the village of Victoria Gracia. Locals suspected Woodroffe of being involved in her murder, despite the lack of evidence linking him to the crime.

As tensions escalated, a video surfaced on social media showing a man, later identified as Woodroffe, being dragged by the neck and eventually left motionless on the ground. The footage sparked widespread outrage, and local authorities launched a search for the Canadian national. On Saturday, Woodroffe's body was discovered in a shallow unmarked grave, bearing signs of strangulation and physical trauma.

Peruvian officials have stated that they are investigating various theories behind Woodroffe's killing, including the possibility that he was mistakenly blamed for Arévalo's murder due to his association with her as a client. The incident highlights the challenges faced by indigenous communities in the Amazon region, where crimes often go unpunished due to the thin police presence. The case has also raised concerns about the increasing popularity of ayahuasca tourism, which has led to a rise in deaths and violent incidents linked to the substance.

Yarrow Willard, a close friend of Woodroffe's, described him as a gentle person on a journey to find enlightenment and deeper meaning. Willard expressed shock and disbelief at the allegations against his friend, stating that Woodroffe was not capable of violence. The incident has sparked a heated debate about the role of outsiders in the Amazon region and the need for greater understanding and cooperation between local communities and law enforcement agencies.

Olivia Arévalo, the murdered spiritual healer, was a respected member of the Shipibo-Conibo group and a vocal advocate for the preservation of indigenous culture and rights. Her killing has sparked widespread condemnation, with many calling for justice and accountability in the region. The case serves as a stark reminder of the complexities and challenges facing indigenous communities in the Amazon, where traditional ways of life are under threat from external pressures and conflicts over land ownership.

history.draft:
In the Peruvian Amazon, a tragic series of events unfolded, resulting in the lynching of a 41-year-old Canadian national, Sebastian Woodroffe, and the murder of an 81-year-old indigenous spiritual healer, Olivia Arévalo. Woodroffe, a resident of Vancouver Island, Canada, had traveled to the Ucayali region of the Peruvian Amazon to pursue an apprenticeship with a plant healer from the Shipibo indigenous group, with the aim of becoming an addiction counselor using hallucinogenic medicine. 

According to friends and acquaintances, Woodroffe had visited the region multiple times to experiment with ayahuasca, a hallucinogenic brew made from native plants, and had expressed a deep respect for the local culture and traditions. However, on Thursday, Arévalo, a respected spiritual healer and rights activist, was shot dead outside her home in the village of Victoria Gracia. Locals suspected Woodroffe of being involved in her murder, despite the lack of evidence linking him to the crime.

As tensions escalated, a video surfaced on social media showing a man, later identified as Woodroffe, being dragged by the neck and eventually left motionless on the ground. The footage sparked widespread outrage, and local authorities launched a search for the Canadian national. On Saturday, Woodroffe's body was discovered in a shallow unmarked grave, bearing signs of strangulation and physical trauma.

Peruvian officials have stated that they are investigating various theories behind Woodroffe's killing, including the possibility that he was mistakenly blamed for Arévalo's murder due to his association with her as a client. The incident highlights the challenges faced by indigenous communities in the Amazon region, where crimes often go unpunished due to the thin police presence. The case has also raised concerns about the increasing popularity of ayahuasca tourism, which has led to a rise in deaths and violent incidents linked to the substance.

Yarrow Willard, a close friend of Woodroffe's, described him as a gentle person on a journey to find enlightenment and deeper meaning. Willard expressed shock and disbelief at the allegations against his friend, stating that Woodroffe was not capable of violence. The incident has sparked a heated debate about the role of outsiders in the Amazon region and the need for greater understanding and cooperation between local communities and law enforcement agencies.

Olivia Arévalo, the murdered spiritual healer, was a respected member of the Shipibo-Conibo group and a vocal advocate for the preservation of indigenous culture and rights. Her killing has sparked widespread condemnation, with many calling for justice and accountability in the region. The case serves as a stark reminder of the complexities and challenges facing indigenous communities in the Amazon, where traditional ways of life are under threat from external pressures and conflicts over land ownership.

reference: – The death of a Canadian man is making headlines for its location, manner, and the murkiness around it. The body of Sebastian Woodroffe was found in a shallow grave Saturday in the Ucayali region of Peru's Amazon by police who began searching for the 41-year-old after a video was posted to social media that showed him being dragged on the ground with a rope around his neck until his body goes limp. One early theory, according to local media and a police officer who spoke with the Guardian, is that he was killed over the murder of Olivia Arévalo, an octogenarian shaman from the Shipibo-Konibo tribe who was shot dead Thursday. Woodroffe's body was buried a little more than half-a-mile from the site where she was killed, and he was thought to be one of her clients. But while one potential motive being investigated is that Arévalo's son owed Woodroffe money, another theory is that the same son owed money to a gang member who pulled the trigger. And the AP says authorities have "backed away" from reports that Woodroffe is even a suspect. The BBC reports Woodroffe had been in Peru previously to try the hallucinogen ayahuasca, and that he was looking to start a new career involving the use of "plant medicine" in drug addiction treatment. The Guardian cites a years-old Indiegogo campaign that helped fund a trip from his Vancouver Island home to Peru; on the campaign page he referenced a relative's alcohol addiction and wrote, "Traditional Detox Centres have a 5-8% success rate. Unacceptable." The CBC reports Woodroffe, who was apparently also killed Thursday, leaves behind a 9-year-old son.

final_summary:
In a move seen as a significant step towards dismantling the decade-old No Child Left Behind education law, the Obama administration announced on Friday that two additional states, Washington and Wisconsin, would be exempted from several key provisions of the law. This decision brings the total number of states granted waivers to 26, leaving only a handful of states still bound by the law's strict requirements. The waivers, which were first introduced in February by the Education Department, allow states to opt out of certain aspects of the law in exchange for promising to implement alternative methods for evaluating student performance and teacher effectiveness.

The 10-year-old No Child Left Behind law, signed into effect by former President George W. Bush, mandated that all students achieve proficiency in reading and math by 2014. However, many educators have deemed this goal unrealistic, leading to widespread criticism of the law's effectiveness. Despite attempts by Congress to reauthorize the law over the past five years, lawmakers have been unable to reach a consensus, prompting the Obama administration to take matters into its own hands.

Education Secretary Arne Duncan has stated that the waivers are a temporary measure intended to address the law's shortcomings until a revised version can be passed. According to Duncan, a bipartisan reauthorization of the Elementary and Secondary Education Act – the formal name of the No Child Left Behind law – remains the preferred course of action. However, with 26 states already granted waivers, the law's impact has been significantly diminished.

Washington state, one of the latest recipients of a waiver, will no longer be required to ensure that all students pass both reading and math tests by 2014. Instead, the state will focus on improving test scores for specific subgroups of students, such as those receiving free or reduced-price meals. Wisconsin, which had initially submitted a waiver application in late May, was granted approval after revising its proposal to provide more detailed information.

Critics argue that the waivers do little to address the underlying issues with the No Child Left Behind law, which has been criticized for prioritizing standardized testing over other important educational factors. Some educators have expressed concerns that the waivers may perpetuate the same problems, as states continue to rely heavily on test scores to evaluate teacher and school performance. Others see the waivers as a necessary step towards reform, allowing states to develop more nuanced approaches to education.

As the debate surrounding the No Child Left Behind law continues, the Obama administration's use of waivers has raised questions about the limits of executive power and the role of Congress in shaping education policy. With additional waivers pending in 10 states and the District of Columbia, the fate of the law remains uncertain, and its eventual replacement or revision hangs in the balance. By 2018, the waiver agreement requires Washington to cut in half the achievement gaps between various ethnic and economic groups, compared to 100 percent passage rates.

reference: – It's a milestone for No Child Left Behind, but not in a good way: More than half of the nation is now exempt. The White House issued waivers today to the 25th and 26th states, and another 10 are in the works, reports the New York Times. The exemptions come ahead of a 2014 deadline requiring all students to be proficient in math and reading. They also come close to making the hallmark of George W. Bush's education reform "essentially nullified," says the Times. Education chief Arne Duncan says the waivers are necessary while the administration works with Congress to rewrite the law, which critics say is too focused on test results, notes AP. "A strong, bipartisan reauthorization of the Elementary and Secondary Education Act remains the best path forward in education reform, but as 26 states have now demonstrated, our kids can't wait any longer for Congress to act," said Duncan in a statement. A school superintendent in Maryland says the waivers don't do much to get away from standardized tests, however. “I have a lot of respect for Arne Duncan, but it’s just sort of moving around the chairs on the Titanic.”

final_summary:
Typhoon Haiyan, one of the most powerful storms ever recorded on land, tore through the Philippines on Friday, leaving a trail of devastation and claiming the lives of at least 2,300 people. The storm, which made landfall in the eastern provinces of Leyte and Samar, brought with it winds of up to 195mph and sea surges of up to 20ft, destroying entire villages and leaving millions without access to basic necessities like food, water, and shelter.

In the aftermath of the disaster, the international community rallied to provide aid to the affected regions. The UK government, led by Prime Minister David Cameron, pledged more than £20m in aid and dispatched the aircraft carrier HMS Illustrious to the Philippines. The carrier was expected to arrive in the Philippines, where it would join the destroyer HMS Daring, which was already en route to the country. The carrier would bring with it helicopters capable of transporting food and water to remote areas, as well as equipment to provide safe drinking water.

The Disasters Emergency Committee (DEC) also launched an appeal, raising £23m in its first 48 hours. The DEC, comprising 14 major UK charities, including Save the Children, worked tirelessly to coordinate the relief efforts, with Save the Children CEO Justin Forsyth stating that the funds raised allowed them to get aid directly into the heart of the catastrophe.

Meanwhile, the US government pledged $20m in humanitarian aid, with the USS George Washington, an aircraft carrier carrying 5,000 crew, expanding search-and-rescue operations and providing a platform for helicopters to transport supplies. The US military also deployed two destroyers and other vessels to the region, with plans to send up to 1,000 troops to assist in the relief efforts.

China, despite facing criticism for its initial pledge, eventually committed to sending 10m yuan ($1.6m) in relief goods. Japan also pledged $10m, including tents and blankets, and promised to send up to 1,000 troops to assist in the relief efforts.

As the relief efforts gained momentum, aid began to trickle into the worst-hit areas, including Tacloban, the capital of Leyte province. However, the situation remained dire, with thousands of people still waiting for assistance. The city's airport, which had been rendered inoperable by the storm, was finally cleared of debris, allowing flights to resume, bringing in much-needed aid and evacuating those in need.

On Wednesday, the BBC reported that the relief operation in the central Philippines was making progress, with more victims receiving help. However, there was still no large-scale food distribution taking place. The first mass burials had been carried out in Tacloban, and the confirmed death toll exceeded 2,300.

Guiuan, a town of 45,000, was heavily damaged by the typhoon, with the French aid charity MSF describing the situation as bleak. However, after earlier problems with looting, some supplies were now getting in. 

In Manila, the Philippine government worked to coordinate the relief efforts, with Cabinet Secretary Rene Almendras assuring that major roads had been cleared of debris and that authorities had reached all but four affected areas to assess the needs. The European Commission pledged €500m in emergency loans and grants, and the Australian government committed A$30m in aid.

Flights delivering aid from around the world were arriving at the airport in Cebu, which had been turned into a logistics hub for the relief effort. The many donations included a field hospital from Belgium and a portable purification plant from Germany, according to European officials. The US had delivered nearly 274,000 pounds of supplies to Tacloban, including plastic tarps, hygiene kits, blankets, and medical supplies.

Despite the challenges, there were glimmers of hope amidst the devastation. In Tacloban, residents came together to share stories and laughter, finding ways to cope with the trauma they had endured. In other areas, aid workers and volunteers worked tirelessly to distribute food, water, and medical supplies, often facing logistical challenges and security concerns.

As the relief efforts continued, concerns grew for other hard-hit communities, such as the port city of Ormoc, which was struggling to receive aid due to fuel shortages and lack of power. The international community remained committed to supporting the Philippines, with the European Commission pledging €500m in emergency loans and grants, and the Australian government committing A$30m in aid.

reference: – Help is finally beginning to reach hundreds of thousands of people struggling to survive in the aftermath of Typhoon Haiyan. The USS George Washington and two US cruisers have arrived in the Philippines and will distribute food, water, and medicine, as well as assist search and rescue operations in what the top US commander in the Philippines tells the BBC will be an American aid effort on an unprecedented scale. US planes are bringing in supplies and a medical ship is on the way; Britain is also sending an aircraft carrier, notes the BBC. In other developments: Supplies from the US and other countries are arriving, but some areas are still in desperate need of relief and "the major challenge is logistics," a European Commission spokesman tells the LA Times. "With all this aid arriving and at the same time, the various Philippine authorities—military, civilian structures, the Philippine Red Cross—trying to distribute aid to so many communities ... obviously there are bottlenecks." Lawlessness in the devastated city of Tacloban is also slowing the distribution of aid, the Guardian finds. A Red Cross convoy was allegedly hijacked by armed men and there are rumors that inmates who escaped during the storm have been ambushing people carrying supplies. Communist rebels have declared a ceasefire in disaster areas, but troops say there has been at least one attack. Some 2,357 people have been confirmed dead, but the toll is expected to rise significantly as remote areas hit hard by the storm are reached. In Tacloban, where bodies are being trucked to mass graves, the city administrator says he is sticking by his original estimate of 10,000 dead in the city alone. In a smaller city south of Tacloban, the devastation is even worse and a 16-member medical team from California is laboring in extreme conditions to help survivors, NBC finds. The volunteer surgeons—working by flashlight in Tanawan's ruined town hall—believe the region will need help for months. "I don’t know when or how we are going to be able to leave," one doctor says. "This is just the beginning of a wave of misery." USA Today has more on that front: The few doctors in Manila are already overwhelmed with hundreds of patients—and those are mostly minor injuries. They're soon expecting to see big problems like pneumonia, diarrhea, infections, and dehydration. And the New York Times looks at a hospital in Tacloban that has no power, where victims lay waiting for care. Food, water, and medical supplies are short; one patient interviewed by the Times says he has not received painkillers, antibiotics, or even an antiseptic for his wounds.

final_summary:
A series of gruesome murders shook the normally tranquil city of Scottsdale, Ariz., leaving behind a trail of death and destruction. The killings began on Thursday, June 4, 2018, with the fatal shooting of Dr. Steven Pitt, a renowned forensic psychiatrist, outside his office in northeast Phoenix. Pitt, known for his work on high-profile cases such as the JonBenet Ramsey investigation, was a respected figure in his field.

The next day, on Friday, two paralegals, Veleria Sharp, 48, and Laura Anderson, 49, were shot at a law firm in downtown Scottsdale. Sharp managed to stumble out of the office and beg for help before collapsing on the street, while Anderson succumbed to her injuries. Hours later, Marshall Levine, a 72-year-old life coach and marriage counselor, was found dead in his office, bringing the total number of fatalities to four.

As investigators analyzed the evidence, they discovered that the first two shootings were connected, and ballistic tests revealed that the same gun was used in each incident. The authorities received over 100 tips, but it wasn't until Saturday evening that they received the crucial information that led them to the prime suspect, Dwight Lamon Jones, a 56-year-old man with a history of domestic violence.

Jones' connection to the victims lay in a bitter divorce proceeding several years ago, in which he had been ordered to see Dr. Pitt as part of his evaluation. His son had also seen a doctor who occupied the same office space as Dr. Levine, leading investigators to believe that Jones had targeted the individuals involved in his divorce case. However, it was unclear why Jones had waited for the past several days to exact revenge.

Connie Jones, Dwight's ex-wife, released a statement expressing her deep sadness and fear for her own safety, describing her former husband as a "very emotionally disturbed person." Her current husband, a retired Phoenix police detective, had recognized the connection between the divorce and the crime scenes and notified the police on Saturday night, providing the crucial tip that led to Jones' identification.

Jones had a history of domestic violence, having been arrested in 2009 on charges of mistreating his wife and child. He had been living in extended-stay hotels for the past nine years, fueling speculation about his motives and state of mind. As the investigation unfolded, police provided security for individuals who may have been at risk, including those in the medical and legal professions.

On Monday, police surrounded an Extended Stay America hotel in Scottsdale, where Jones was believed to be hiding. After a brief standoff, Jones opened fire on the officers, but ultimately took his own life with a self-inflicted gunshot wound. The investigation revealed that Jones had also been linked to two additional killings in Fountain Hills, a nearby affluent suburb, bringing the total number of fatalities to six in approximately 96 hours.

Rich Slavin, an assistant chief with the Scottsdale Police Department, described Jones' actions as "the worst of humanity," while Phoenix Police Chief Jeri Williams emphasized that taking innocent lives should never make sense. The community was left reeling from the shocking events, with residents expressing shock and horror at the unprecedented level of violence in their usually peaceful neighborhood.

The killings began on Thursday, June 4, 2018, with the fatal shooting of Dr. Steven Pitt, a renowned forensic psychiatrist, outside his office in northeast Phoenix. The next day, on Friday, two paralegals, Veleria Sharp, 48, and Laura Anderson, 49, were shot at a law firm in downtown Scottsdale. Sharp managed to stumble out of the office and beg for help before collapsing on the street, while Anderson succumbed to her injuries. Hours later, Marshall Levine, a 72-year-old life coach and marriage counselor, was found dead in his office, bringing the total number of fatalities to four.

As investigators analyzed the evidence, they discovered that the first two shootings were connected, and ballistic tests revealed that the same gun was used in each incident. The authorities received over 100 tips, but it wasn't until Saturday evening that they received the crucial information that led them to the prime suspect, Dwight Lamon Jones, a 56-year-old man with a history of domestic violence.

Jones' connection to the victims lay in a bitter divorce proceeding several years ago, in which he had been ordered to see Dr. Pitt as part of his evaluation. His son had also seen a doctor who occupied the same office space as Dr. Levine, leading investigators to believe that Jones had targeted the individuals involved in his divorce case. However, it was unclear why Jones had waited for the past several days to exact revenge.

Connie Jones, Dwight's ex-wife, released a statement expressing her deep sadness and fear for her own safety, describing her former husband as a "very emotionally disturbed person." Her current husband, a retired Phoenix police detective, had recognized the connection between the divorce and the crime scenes and notified the police on Saturday night, providing the crucial tip that led to Jones' identification.

Jones had a history of domestic violence, having been arrested in 2009 on charges of mistreating his wife and child. He had been living in extended-stay hotels for the past nine years, fueling speculation about his motives and state of mind. As the investigation unfolded, police provided security for individuals who may have been at risk, including those in the medical and legal professions.

On Monday, police surrounded an Extended Stay America hotel in Scottsdale, where Jones was believed to be hiding. After a brief standoff, Jones opened fire on the officers, but ultimately took his own life with a self-inflicted gunshot wound. The investigation revealed that Jones had also been linked to two additional killings in Fountain Hills, a nearby affluent suburb, bringing the total number of fatalities to six in approximately 96 hours.

Rich Slavin, an assistant chief with the Scottsdale Police Department, described Jones' actions as "the worst of humanity," while Phoenix Police Chief Jeri Williams emphasized that taking innocent lives should never make sense. The community was left reeling from the shocking events, with residents expressing shock and horror at the unprecedented level of violence in their usually peaceful neighborhood.

reference: – A suspect who killed himself as police closed in Monday is now believed to have killed six people in the Phoenix area, police say—and at least four of them were linked to his bitter 2010 divorce. Police believe 56-year-old Dwight Lamon Jones, who shot himself at a Scottsdale motel early Monday, killed all six people in around 96 hours starting early Thursday, the New York Times reports. Police say Jones started the spree when he killed prominent psychiatrist Steven Pitt, whom he'd been required to see as part of divorce proceedings. On Friday, he allegedly killed Veleria Sharp and Laura Anderson, two paralegals at the firm his wife used during the divorce. Hours later Jones allegedly killed Marshall Levine, a marriage counselor who used the same office as a counselor Jones' son saw during the divorce. Police believe Jones also killed Mary Simmons, 70, and Bryon Thomas, 72, in Fountain Hills, around 15 miles away from the other shootings, but their connection to him is still unclear. Jones left around 10 hours of YouTube video in which he outlines an alleged conspiracy to steal custody of his son, who's now 21 years old, the Arizona Republic reports. Ex-wife Connie Jones says Jones was a "very emotionally disturbed person" and she divorced him after he hit her and threatened to kill her. She was granted sole custody of their son. The AP reports that Jones says her new husband, a retired police detective, was the one who made the connection between the divorce and the shootings and told police of his suspicions.

final_summary:
Late Wednesday, Houston Police Department Chief Art Acevedo announced that Joseph James Pappas, a 62-year-old man, was being sought in connection with the fatal shooting of Dr. Mark Hausknecht, a prominent cardiologist who was gunned down while riding his bicycle to work at Houston Methodist Hospital on July 20. According to Acevedo, Pappas' motive for the crime may have been a 20-year-old grudge stemming from his mother's death, which occurred while she was under Hausknecht's care.

Pappas, a former constable deputy who worked for Harris County Constable Precincts 2 and 7 for approximately 30 years, had been missing for 36 to 48 hours prior to the announcement, and his last known communication was a Tuesday morning text message indicating he intended to take his own life. Despite this, police warned that Pappas should be considered armed, dangerous, and possibly suicidal.

A tip on Tuesday led investigators to suspect Pappas, although the nature of the tip was not disclosed. Upon searching his home, police discovered evidence linking him to the crime, although the specifics of this evidence were not revealed. Pappas was described as white and physically fit, and was believed to be traveling on his 10-speed bicycle, which he reportedly rode extensively and almost exclusively.

Dr. Hausknecht, a renowned cardiologist who had treated former President George H.W. Bush in 2000 for an irregular heartbeat, was remembered as a humble and generous man who was well-respected by his patients and colleagues. The Associated Press reported that the Houston Police Department released a photograph of Pappas, urging the public to come forward with any information regarding his whereabouts.

As the investigation continued, police acknowledged that Pappas had recently attempted to sell various firearms and tactical gear online, listing his phone number and a Houston address associated with his real estate business. While it was unclear if these items were connected to the crime, authorities emphasized that they were exploring all aspects of Pappas' life and activities.

In response to the tragic event, the Texas Medical Association expressed concern about the potential for grudge-driven violence against healthcare professionals, citing a 2012 study highlighting instances of determined shooters targeting medical staff. The association noted that such incidents can stem from deep-seated emotions and a desire for retribution, particularly in cases where individuals feel that a loved one has suffered due to medical malpractice or negligence.

As the search for Pappas continued, the Houston community mourned the loss of Dr. Hausknecht, who was remembered for his dedication to his patients and his commitment to volunteering in the community. The case remained under investigation, with authorities urging caution and vigilance in the pursuit of bringing Pappas to justice.
